**Los Angeles**[[b]](#cite_note-16) (often referred to by its initials, **LA**) is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_California "List of municipalities in California") in the U.S. state of [California](/wiki/California "California"), and the commercial, [financial](/wiki/Financial_District,_Los_Angeles "Financial District, Los Angeles"), and [cultural](/wiki/Culture_of_Los_Angeles "Culture of Los Angeles") center of [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"). With an estimated 3.88 million residents within the city limits as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles&action=edit),[[8]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-8) it is the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States, behind [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and the largest city in the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States"). The city has an [ethnically](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Los_Angeles "Ethnic groups in Los Angeles") and culturally diverse population, and is the principal city of a [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_areas "Metropolitan statistical areas") of 12.9 million people (2024). [Greater Los Angeles](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles"), a [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") that includes the Los Angeles and [Riverside–San Bernardino](/wiki/Riverside%E2%80%93San_Bernardino "Riverside–San Bernardino") [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area"), is a sprawling [metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis "Metropolis") of over 18.5 million residents.

The majority of the city proper lies in the [Los Angeles Basin](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Basin "Los Angeles Basin") adjacent to the Pacific Ocean in the west and extending partly through the [Santa Monica Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Mountains "Santa Monica Mountains") and north into the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley"), with the city bordering the [San Gabriel Valley](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Valley "San Gabriel Valley") to its east. It covers about 469 square miles (1,210 km2),[[6]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2021-6) and is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") and most populated city of [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California"), which is the [most populous county in the United States](/wiki/List_of_the_most_populous_counties_in_the_United_States "List of the most populous counties in the United States") with an estimated 9.86 million residents as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles&action=edit).[[15]](#cite_note-LosAngelesCountyDecline2022-17) It is the [third-most visited city in the U.S.](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_United_States "Tourism in the United States") (after New York City and [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami")), with over 2.7 million visitors as of 2023.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

The area that became Los Angeles was originally inhabited by the [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_people_of_California "Indigenous people of California") [Tongva people](/wiki/Tongva "Tongva") and later claimed by [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo") for [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in 1542. The city was founded on September 4, 1781, under Spanish governor [Felipe de Neve](/wiki/Felipe_de_Neve "Felipe de Neve"), on the village of [Yaanga](/wiki/Yaanga "Yaanga").[[17]](#cite_note-:02-19) It became a part of the [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire") in 1821 following the [Mexican War of Independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence"). In 1848, at the end of the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), Los Angeles and the rest of California were purchased as part of the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") and became part of the United States. Los Angeles was [incorporated](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") as a municipality on April 4, 1850, five months before California achieved [statehood](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"). The discovery of [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") in the 1890s brought rapid growth to the city.[[18]](#cite_note-20) The city was further expanded with the completion of the [Los Angeles Aqueduct](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Aqueduct "Los Angeles Aqueduct") in 1913, which delivers water from [Eastern California](/wiki/Eastern_California "Eastern California").

Los Angeles has a diverse economy with a broad range of industries. It is the global hub of film and television production,[[19]](#cite_note-variety2025-21) though its share has suffered historic declines since the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[20]](#cite_note-HollywoodLosAngelesExodus1-22) It has [one of the busiest container ports](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles") in the Americas,[[21]](#cite_note-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25) and despite a business exodus from [downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles"), the city's urban core is evolving as a cultural center with the world's largest showcase of architecture designed by [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry").[[24]](#cite_note-DowntownLosAngelesHurting-26) In 2024, the [Los Angeles metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles") had a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") of over $1.295 trillion,[[25]](#cite_note-fred-27) making it the city with the [third-largest GDP](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP") in the world, after [New York](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") and [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo_metropolitan_area "Tokyo metropolitan area"). Los Angeles hosted the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") in [1932](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics") and [1984](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics"), and will also host in [2028](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics").

## Toponymy

See also: [Etymology of place names in Los Angeles County, California](/wiki/Etymology_of_place_names_in_Los_Angeles_County,_California "Etymology of place names in Los Angeles County, California")

On September 4, 1781, a group of 44 settlers known as "[Los Pobladores](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Pobladores "Los Angeles Pobladores")" founded the pueblo (town) they called [El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles](/wiki/El_Pueblo_de_Nuestra_Se%C3%B1ora_la_Reina_de_los_%C3%81ngeles "El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles"), 'The Town of [Our Lady](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") the Queen of the Angels'.[[26]](#cite_note-almanac-28) The original name of the settlement is disputed; the [Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records") rendered it as "El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles del Río [Porciúncula](/wiki/Portiuncula "Portiuncula")"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]; other sources have shortened or alternate versions of the longer name.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)

The local English pronunciation of the name of the city has varied over time. A 1953 article in the [journal](/wiki/Names_(journal) "Names (journal)") of the [American Name Society](/wiki/American_Name_Society "American Name Society") asserts that the pronunciation [/lɔːs ˈændʒələs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*lawss AN-jəl-əs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") was established following the 1850 incorporation of the city and that since the 1880s the pronunciation [/loʊs ˈæŋɡələs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*lohss ANG-gəl-əs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") emerged from a trend in California to give places Spanish, or Spanish-sounding, names and pronunciations.[[29]](#cite_note-stein-31) In 1908, librarian [Charles Fletcher Lummis](/wiki/Charles_Fletcher_Lummis "Charles Fletcher Lummis"), who argued for the name's pronunciation with a hard *g* ([/ɡ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")),[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) reported that there were at least 12 pronunciation variants.[[32]](#cite_note-34) In the early 1900s, the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* advocated for pronouncing it *Loce AHNG-hayl-ais* ([/loʊs ˈɑːŋheɪleɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), approximating Spanish [[los ˈaŋxeles]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), by printing the [respelling](/wiki/Respelling "Respelling") under its masthead for several years.[[33]](#cite_note-harvey-35) This did not find favor.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

Since the 1930s, [/lɔːs ˈændʒələs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") has been most common.[[35]](#cite_note-37) In 1934, the [United States Board on Geographic Names](/wiki/United_States_Board_on_Geographic_Names "United States Board on Geographic Names") decreed that this pronunciation be used by the federal government.[[33]](#cite_note-harvey-35) This was also endorsed in 1952 by a "jury" appointed by Mayor [Fletcher Bowron](/wiki/Fletcher_Bowron "Fletcher Bowron") to devise an official pronunciation.[[29]](#cite_note-stein-31)[[33]](#cite_note-harvey-35)

Common pronunciations in the United Kingdom include [/lɒs ˈændʒɪliːz, -lɪz, -lɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*loss AN-jil-eez, -⁠iz, -⁠iss*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key").[[36]](#cite_note-38) Phonetician [Jack Windsor Lewis](/wiki/Jack_Windsor_Lewis "Jack Windsor Lewis") described the most common one, [/lɒs ˈændʒɪliːz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Los_Angeles.ogg "File:En-uk-Los Angeles.ogg"), as a [spelling pronunciation](/wiki/Spelling_pronunciation "Spelling pronunciation") based on analogy to Greek words ending in *-‍es*, "reflecting a time when the classics were familiar if Spanish was not".[[37]](#cite_note-39)

## History

Main article: [History of Los Angeles](/wiki/History_of_Los_Angeles "History of Los Angeles")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Los Angeles](/wiki/Timeline_of_Los_Angeles "Timeline of Los Angeles").

For a topical guide, see [Outline of the history of Los Angeles](/wiki/Outline_of_the_history_of_Los_Angeles "Outline of the history of Los Angeles").

### Indigenous history

The settlement of [Indigenous Californians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_California "Indigenous peoples of California") in the modern [Los Angeles Basin](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Basin "Los Angeles Basin") and the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley") was dominated by the [Tongva](/wiki/Tongva "Tongva") (now also known as the *Gabrieleño* since the era of Spanish colonization). The historic center of Tongva power in the region was the settlement of [Yaanga](/wiki/Yaanga "Yaanga") ([Tongva](/wiki/Tongva_language "Tongva language"): *Iyáangẚ*), meaning "place of the [poison oak](/wiki/Toxicodendron_diversilobum "Toxicodendron diversilobum")", which would one day be the site where the Spanish founded the [Pueblo de Los Ángeles](/wiki/Pueblo_de_Los_%C3%81ngeles "Pueblo de Los Ángeles"). *Iyáangẚ* has also been translated as "the valley of smoke".[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-google86-42)[[41]](#cite_note-sfgate2002-43)[[17]](#cite_note-:02-19)

### Spanish rule

Maritime explorer [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo") claimed the area of southern California for the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in 1542, while on an official military exploring expedition, as he was moving northward along the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") coast from earlier colonizing bases of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") in [Central](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and South America.[[42]](#cite_note-44) [Gaspar de Portolà](/wiki/Gaspar_de_Portol%C3%A0 "Gaspar de Portolà") and [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") missionary [Juan Crespí](/wiki/Juan_Cresp%C3%AD "Juan Crespí") reached the present site of Los Angeles on August 2, 1769.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Mission_San_Fernando_Rey_de_Espa%C3%B1a_%28Oriana_Day%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Mission_San_Fernando_Rey_de_Espa%C3%B1a_%28Oriana_Day%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mission_San_Fernando_Rey_de_Espa%C3%B1a_(Oriana_Day)_(cropped).jpg)

The [Spanish](/wiki/Empire_of_Spain "Empire of Spain") founded [Mission San Fernando Rey de España](/wiki/Mission_San_Fernando_Rey_de_Espa%C3%B1a "Mission San Fernando Rey de España") in 1797.

In 1771, Franciscan [friar](/wiki/Friar "Friar") [Junípero Serra](/wiki/Jun%C3%ADpero_Serra "Junípero Serra") directed the building of the [Mission San Gabriel Arcángel](/wiki/Mission_San_Gabriel_Arc%C3%A1ngel "Mission San Gabriel Arcángel"), the first [mission](/wiki/Mission_(station) "Mission (station)") in the area.[[44]](#cite_note-LeffingwellWorden2005-46) On September 4, 1781, a group of 44 settlers known as "[Los Pobladores](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Pobladores "Los Angeles Pobladores")" founded the pueblo (town) they called [El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles](/wiki/El_Pueblo_de_Nuestra_Se%C3%B1ora_la_Reina_de_los_%C3%81ngeles "El Pueblo de Nuestra Señora la Reina de los Ángeles"), 'The Town of Our Lady the Queen of the Angels'.[[26]](#cite_note-almanac-28) The present-day city has the largest [Roman Catholic archdiocese](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Los_Angeles "Archdiocese of Los Angeles") in the United States. Two-thirds of the Mexican or ([New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain")) settlers were [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") or [mulatto](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto"), a mixture of African, indigenous, and European ancestry.[[45]](#cite_note-47) The settlement remained a small ranch town for decades, but by 1820, the population had increased to about 650 residents.[[46]](#cite_note-48) Today, the pueblo is commemorated in the historic district of [Los Angeles Pueblo Plaza](/wiki/El_Pueblo_de_Los_%C3%81ngeles_Historical_Monument "El Pueblo de Los Ángeles Historical Monument") and [Olvera Street](/wiki/Olvera_Street "Olvera Street"), the oldest part of Los Angeles.[[47]](#cite_note-Estrada2006-49)

### Mexican rule

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Portrait_of_Pio_Pico_%28Californian_State_Library%29_%28detail%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Pio_Pico_%28Californian_State_Library%29_%28detail%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Pio_Pico_(Californian_State_Library)_(detail).jpg)

[Californio](/wiki/Californio "Californio") statesman [Pío Pico](/wiki/P%C3%ADo_Pico "Pío Pico"), who served as the last [Mexican governor of California](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_California_before_1850 "List of governors of California before 1850"), played an influential role in the development of Los Angeles in the late Mexican and early American eras.

[New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") achieved its independence from the Spanish Empire in 1821, and the pueblo now existed within the new [Mexican Republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic"). During Mexican rule, Governor [Pío Pico](/wiki/P%C3%ADo_Pico "Pío Pico") made Los Angeles the regional capital of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California").[[48]](#cite_note-50) By this time, the new republic introduced more [secularization](/wiki/Mexican_secularization_act_of_1833 "Mexican secularization act of 1833") acts within the Los Angeles region.[[49]](#cite_note-51) In 1846, during the wider [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), marines from the United States occupied the pueblo. This resulted in the [siege of Los Angeles](/wiki/Siege_of_Los_Angeles "Siege of Los Angeles"), where 150 Mexican militias fought the occupiers, who eventually surrendered.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

Mexican rule ended following the American [Conquest of California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California"), part of the larger [Mexican-American War](/wiki/Mexican-American_War "Mexican-American War"). Americans took control from the [Californios](/wiki/Californios "Californios") after a series of battles, culminating with the signing of the [Treaty of Cahuenga](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cahuenga "Treaty of Cahuenga") on January 13, 1847.[[51]](#cite_note-53) The [Mexican Cession](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession") was formalized in the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848, which ceded Los Angeles and the rest of Alta California to the United States.

### Post-Conquest era

See also: [Victorian Downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Victorian_Downtown_Los_Angeles "Victorian Downtown Los Angeles") and [Los Angeles in the 1920s](/wiki/Los_Angeles_in_the_1920s "Los Angeles in the 1920s")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Treaty_of_Cahuenga.jpg/330px-Treaty_of_Cahuenga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Cahuenga.jpg)

The [Treaty of Cahuenga](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cahuenga "Treaty of Cahuenga"), signed in 1847 by Californio [Andrés Pico](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_Pico "Andrés Pico") and American [John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont"), ended the U.S. [Conquest of California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California").

[Railroads arrived](/wiki/History_of_Los_Angeles#Railroads "History of Los Angeles") with the completion of the transcontinental [Southern Pacific](/wiki/Southern_Pacific_Railroad "Southern Pacific Railroad") line from [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") to Los Angeles in 1876 and the [Santa Fe Railroad](/wiki/Atchison,_Topeka_and_Santa_Fe_Railway "Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway") in 1885.[[52]](#cite_note-Mulholland2002-54) Petroleum was discovered in the city and surrounding area in 1892, and by 1923, the discoveries had helped [California](/wiki/California "California") become the country's largest oil producer, accounting for about one-quarter of the world's petroleum output.[[53]](#cite_note-Kipen2011-55)

By 1900, the population had grown to more than 102,000,[[54]](#cite_note-56) putting pressure on the city's [water supply](/wiki/Water_supply "Water supply").[[55]](#cite_note-57) The completion of the [Los Angeles Aqueduct](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Aqueduct "Los Angeles Aqueduct") in 1913, under the supervision of [William Mulholland](/wiki/William_Mulholland "William Mulholland"), ensured the continued growth of the city.[[56]](#cite_note-Reisner1993-58) Because of clauses in the city's charter that prevented the City of Los Angeles from selling or providing water from the aqueduct to any area outside its borders, many adjacent cities and communities felt compelled to join Los Angeles.[[57]](#cite_note-LAgrowth-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-FinalReport-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Paramount_Pictures_studio_gate%2C_c._1940_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Paramount_Pictures_studio_gate%2C_c._1940_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paramount_Pictures_studio_gate,_c._1940_(cropped).jpg)

In the early 20th century, [Hollywood studios](/wiki/Major_film_studios "Major film studios"), like [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), helped transform [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") into the world capital of film and helped solidify LA as a global economic hub.

Los Angeles created the first municipal [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning") ordinance in the United States. On September 14, 1908, the [Los Angeles City Council](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Council,_1889%E2%80%931909 "Los Angeles City Council, 1889–1909") promulgated residential and industrial land use zones. The new ordinance established three residential zones of a single type, where industrial uses were prohibited. The proscriptions included barns, lumber yards, and any industrial land use employing machine-powered equipment. These laws were enforced against industrial properties after the fact. These prohibitions were in addition to existing activities that were already regulated as nuisances. These included explosives warehousing, gas works, oil drilling, slaughterhouses, and [tanneries](/wiki/Tannery "Tannery"). The Los Angeles City Council also designated seven industrial zones within the city. However, between 1908 and 1915, the Los Angeles City Council created various exceptions to the broad proscriptions that applied to these three residential zones, and as a consequence, some industrial uses emerged within them. There are two differences between the 1908 Residence District Ordinance and later zoning laws in the United States. First, the 1908 laws did not establish a comprehensive zoning map as the [1916 New York City Zoning Ordinance](/wiki/Zoning_in_the_United_States#Origins_and_history "Zoning in the United States") did. Second, the residential zones did not distinguish types of housing; they treated apartments, hotels, and detached single-family housing equally.[[60]](#cite_note-weiss80-62)

In 1910, Hollywood merged into Los Angeles, with 10 movie companies already operating in the city at the time. By 1921, more than 80 percent of the world's film industry was concentrated in L.A.[[61]](#cite_note-Buntin2010-63) The money generated by the industry kept the city insulated from much of the economic loss suffered by the rest of the country during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States").[[62]](#cite_note-YoungYoung2007-64)
By 1930, the population surpassed one million.[[63]](#cite_note-65) In 1932, the city hosted the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics").

### Post–World War II

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/WSA_Photo_4235.jpg/250px-WSA_Photo_4235.jpg)](/wiki/File:WSA_Photo_4235.jpg)

The [California Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/California_Shipbuilding_Corporation "California Shipbuilding Corporation") on [Terminal Island](/wiki/Terminal_Island "Terminal Island") was among the many builders that made the [Port of Los Angeles](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles") one of the country's largest [shipyards](/wiki/Shipyards "Shipyards") during World War II.

During the [Second World War](/wiki/United_States_home_front_during_World_War_II "United States home front during World War II"), Los Angeles was a major center of wartime manufacturing, such as shipbuilding and aircraft. [Calship](/wiki/California_Shipbuilding_Corporation "California Shipbuilding Corporation") built hundreds of [Liberty Ships](/wiki/Liberty_Ship "Liberty Ship") and [Victory Ships](/wiki/Victory_Ship "Victory Ship") on Terminal Island, and the Los Angeles area was the headquarters of six of the country's major aircraft manufacturers ([Douglas Aircraft Company](/wiki/Douglas_Aircraft_Company "Douglas Aircraft Company"), [Hughes Aircraft](/wiki/Hughes_Aircraft_Company "Hughes Aircraft Company"), [Lockheed](/wiki/Lockheed_Corporation "Lockheed Corporation"), [North American Aviation](/wiki/North_American_Aviation "North American Aviation"), [Northrop Corporation](/wiki/Northrop_Corporation "Northrop Corporation"), and [Vultee](/wiki/Vultee_Aircraft "Vultee Aircraft")). During the war, more aircraft were produced in one year than in all the pre-war years since the Wright brothers flew the first airplane in 1903, combined. Manufacturing in Los Angeles skyrocketed, and as [William S. Knudsen](/wiki/William_S._Knudsen "William S. Knudsen"), of the National Defense Advisory Commission, put it, "We won because we smothered the enemy in an avalanche of production, the like of which he had never seen, nor dreamed possible."[[64]](#cite_note-66)

After the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") Los Angeles grew more rapidly than ever, [sprawling](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") northwest into the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley").[[65]](#cite_note-Bruegmann2006-67) The expansion of the state owned [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") during the 1950s and 1960s helped propel suburban growth and signaled the demise of the city's privately owned [electrified rail system](/wiki/Pacific_Electric "Pacific Electric"), once the world's largest.

As a consequence of World War II, suburban growth, and population density, many amusement parks were built and operated in this area.[[66]](#cite_note-Braun-68) An example is [Beverly Park](/wiki/Beverly_Park_(amusement_park) "Beverly Park (amusement park)"), which was located at the corner of Beverly Boulevard and La Cienega before being closed and replaced by the [Beverly Center](/wiki/Beverly_Center "Beverly Center").[[67]](#cite_note-Jennings-69)

In the second half of the 20th century, Los Angeles substantially reduced the amount of housing that could be built by drastically [downzoning](/wiki/Zoning_in_the_United_States "Zoning in the United States") the city. In 1960, the city had a total zoned capacity for approximately 10 million people. By 1990, that capacity had fallen to 4.5 million as a result of policy decisions to ban housing through zoning.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

Racial tensions led to the [Watts riots](/wiki/Watts_riots "Watts riots") in 1965, resulting in 34 deaths and over 1,000 injuries.[[69]](#cite_note-Hinton2-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Olympic_Torch_Tower_of_the_Los_Angeles_Coliseum.jpg/250px-Olympic_Torch_Tower_of_the_Los_Angeles_Coliseum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olympic_Torch_Tower_of_the_Los_Angeles_Coliseum.jpg)

Opening ceremony of the [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") at the [LA Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum")

In 1969, California became the birthplace of the Internet, as the first [ARPANET](/wiki/ARPANET "ARPANET") transmission was sent from the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") (UCLA) to the [Stanford Research Institute](/wiki/Stanford_Research_Institute "Stanford Research Institute") in [Menlo Park](/wiki/Menlo_Park,_California "Menlo Park, California").[[70]](#cite_note-HafnerLyon1999-72)

In 1973, [Tom Bradley](/wiki/Tom_Bradley_(American_politician) "Tom Bradley (American politician)") was elected as the city's first African American mayor, serving for five terms until retiring in 1993. Other events in the city during the 1970s included the [Symbionese Liberation Army](/wiki/Symbionese_Liberation_Army "Symbionese Liberation Army")'s [South Central](/wiki/South_Los_Angeles "South Los Angeles") standoff in 1974 and the [Hillside Stranglers](/wiki/Hillside_Stranglers "Hillside Stranglers") murder cases in 1977–1978.[[71]](#cite_note-vronsky187-73)

In early 1984, the city surpassed Chicago in population, thus becoming the second-largest city in the United States.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

In 1984, the city hosted the Summer Olympic Games [for the second time](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics"). Despite being [boycotted by 14 Communist countries](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1984 Summer Olympics boycott"), the 1984 Olympics became more financially successful than any previous,[[73]](#cite_note-75) and the second Olympics to turn a profit; the other, according to an analysis of contemporary newspaper reports, was the [1932 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics"), also held in Los Angeles.[[74]](#cite_note-Zarnowski-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/48_California_Willshire_Grand_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-48_California_Willshire_Grand_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:48_California_Willshire_Grand_(cropped).jpg)

[Wilshire Grand Center](/wiki/Wilshire_Grand_Center "Wilshire Grand Center"), built in 2017, is the [tallest building in California](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_California "List of tallest buildings in California") and in the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States").

Racial tensions erupted on April 29, 1992, with the acquittal by a [Simi Valley](/wiki/Simi_Valley "Simi Valley") jury of four [Los Angeles Police Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") (LAPD) officers captured on videotape beating [Rodney King](/wiki/Rodney_King "Rodney King"), culminating in [large-scale riots](/wiki/Los_Angeles_riots_of_1992 "Los Angeles riots of 1992").[[75]](#cite_note-RuckerUpton2007-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78)

In 1994, the magnitude 6.7 [Northridge earthquake](/wiki/Northridge_earthquake "Northridge earthquake") shook the city, causing $12.5 billion in damage and 72 deaths.[[77]](#cite_note-79) The century ended with the [Rampart scandal](/wiki/Rampart_scandal "Rampart scandal"), one of the most extensive documented cases of police misconduct in American history.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

### 21st century

In 2002, Mayor [James Hahn](/wiki/James_Hahn "James Hahn") led the campaign against secession, resulting in voters defeating efforts by the San Fernando Valley and Hollywood to secede from the city.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

In [2022](/wiki/2022_Los_Angeles_mayoral_election "2022 Los Angeles mayoral election"), [Karen Bass](/wiki/Karen_Bass "Karen Bass") became the city's first female [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Los_Angeles "Mayor of Los Angeles"), making Los Angeles the largest U.S. city to have ever had a woman as mayor.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

In January 2025, a series of [devastating wildfires](/wiki/January_2025_Southern_California_wildfires "January 2025 Southern California wildfires") caused by severe winds swept through Southern California, with the [Pacific Palisades fire](/wiki/Palisades_Fire "Palisades Fire") causing widespread destruction in Los Angeles's northwestern community of [Pacific Palisades](/wiki/Pacific_Palisades,_Los_Angeles "Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles"), with many calling it the most destructive in the history of the city of Los Angeles.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

In June 2025, the city experienced [protests and riots](/wiki/June_2025_Los_Angeles_protests "June 2025 Los Angeles protests") following raids by [United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement](/wiki/United_States_Immigration_and_Customs_Enforcement "United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement") (ICE).[[82]](#cite_note-84) The Trump administration has targeted Los Angeles over its sanctuary city status, [sending in hundreds of federal agents](/wiki/2025_deployment_of_federal_forces_in_the_United_States "2025 deployment of federal forces in the United States") to apprehend undocumented immigrants.[[83]](#cite_note-85) Trump also dispatched the Army National Guard and US Marines without the consent of local governments.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Los Angeles will host the [2028 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics") and [Paralympic Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Games "Paralympic Games"), making Los Angeles the third city to host the Olympics three times.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88)

## Geography

See also: [Los Angeles Basin](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Basin "Los Angeles Basin") and [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley")

### Topography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Los_Angeles_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2019-03-30.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2019-03-30.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_by_Sentinel-2,_2019-03-30.jpg)

Satellite view of Los Angeles

The city of Los Angeles covers a total area of 502.7 square miles (1,302 km2), comprising 468.7 square miles (1,214 km2) of land and 34.0 square miles (88 km2) of water.[[87]](#cite_note-gazetteer-89) The city extends for 44 miles (71 km) from north to south and for 29 miles (47 km) from east to west. The perimeter of the city is 342 miles (550 km).

Los Angeles is both flat and hilly. The highest point in the city proper is [Mount Lukens](/wiki/Mount_Lukens "Mount Lukens") at 5,074 ft (1,547 m),[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) located in the foothills of the [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains "San Gabriel Mountains") at the north extent of the [Crescenta Valley](/wiki/Crescenta_Valley "Crescenta Valley"). The eastern end of the [Santa Monica Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Mountains "Santa Monica Mountains") stretches from [Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles") to the Pacific Ocean and separates the Los Angeles Basin from the San Fernando Valley. Other hilly parts of Los Angeles include the [Mt. Washington](/wiki/Mt._Washington,_Los_Angeles "Mt. Washington, Los Angeles") area north of Downtown, eastern parts such as [Boyle Heights](/wiki/Boyle_Heights "Boyle Heights"), the [Crenshaw district](/wiki/Crenshaw,_Los_Angeles "Crenshaw, Los Angeles") around the [Baldwin Hills](/wiki/Baldwin_Hills,_Los_Angeles "Baldwin Hills, Los Angeles"), and the [San Pedro](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles "San Pedro, Los Angeles") district.

Surrounding the city are much higher mountains. Immediately to the north lie the [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains "San Gabriel Mountains"), which is a popular recreation area for Angelenos. Its high point is [Mount San Antonio](/wiki/Mount_San_Antonio "Mount San Antonio"), locally known as Mount Baldy, which reaches 10,064 feet (3,068 m). Further afield, the highest point in southern California is [San Gorgonio Mountain](/wiki/San_Gorgonio_Mountain "San Gorgonio Mountain"), 81 miles (130 km) east of downtown Los Angeles,[[90]](#cite_note-92) with a height of 11,503 feet (3,506 m).

The [Los Angeles River](/wiki/Los_Angeles_River "Los Angeles River"), which is largely seasonal, is the primary [drainage channel](/wiki/Drainage_channel "Drainage channel"). It was straightened and lined in 51 miles (82 km) of concrete by the [Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Engineers") to act as a flood control channel.[[91]](#cite_note-Gumprecht2001-93) The river begins in the [Canoga Park](/wiki/Canoga_Park,_Los_Angeles "Canoga Park, Los Angeles") district of the city, flows east from the San Fernando Valley along the north edge of the Santa Monica Mountains, and turns south through the city center, flowing to its mouth in the Port of [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California") at the Pacific Ocean. The smaller [Ballona Creek](/wiki/Ballona_Creek "Ballona Creek") flows into the [Santa Monica Bay](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Bay "Santa Monica Bay") at [Playa del Rey](/wiki/Playa_del_Rey,_Los_Angeles "Playa del Rey, Los Angeles").

### Vegetation

See also: [California coastal sage and chaparral](/wiki/California_coastal_sage_and_chaparral "California coastal sage and chaparral")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/The_Duck_Pond_at_Playa_del_Rey.jpg/250px-The_Duck_Pond_at_Playa_del_Rey.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Duck_Pond_at_Playa_del_Rey.jpg)

[Del Rey Lagoon](/wiki/Del_Rey_Lagoon_Park "Del Rey Lagoon Park") in [Playa del Rey](/wiki/Playa_del_Rey "Playa del Rey")

Los Angeles is rich in native plant species partly because of its diversity of habitats, including beaches, [wetlands](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland"), and mountains. The most prevalent plant communities are [coastal sage scrub](/wiki/Coastal_sage_scrub "Coastal sage scrub"), [chaparral](/wiki/Chaparral "Chaparral") shrubland, and [riparian woodland](/wiki/Riparian_woodland "Riparian woodland").[[92]](#cite_note-Miller2008-94) Native plants include: the [California poppy](/wiki/California_poppy "California poppy"), [matilija poppy](/wiki/Matilija_poppy "Matilija poppy"), [toyon](/wiki/Heteromeles "Heteromeles"), [Ceanothus](/wiki/Ceanothus "Ceanothus"), [Chamise](/wiki/Chamise "Chamise"), [Coast Live Oak](/wiki/Coast_Live_Oak "Coast Live Oak"), [sycamore](/wiki/Platanus_racemosa "Platanus racemosa"), [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow") and [Giant Wildrye](/wiki/Leymus_condensatus "Leymus condensatus"). Many of these native species, such as the [Los Angeles sunflower](/wiki/Helianthus_nuttallii "Helianthus nuttallii"), have become so rare as to be considered endangered. [Mexican Fan Palms](/wiki/Washingtonia_filifera_var._robusta "Washingtonia filifera var. robusta"), [Canary Island Palms](/wiki/Phoenix_canariensis "Phoenix canariensis"), [Queen Palms](/wiki/Syagrus_romanzoffiana "Syagrus romanzoffiana"), [Date Palms](/wiki/Date_Palm "Date Palm"), and [California Fan Palms](/wiki/Washingtonia_filifera "Washingtonia filifera") are common in the Los Angeles area, although only the last is native to California, though still not native to the City of Los Angeles.

Los Angeles has several official flora:

* the official tree of Los Angeles is the Coral Tree (*[Erythrina afra](/wiki/Erythrina_afra "Erythrina afra")*)[[93]](#cite_note-Innovation1979-95)
* the official flower is the Bird of Paradise (*[Strelitzia reginae](/wiki/Strelitzia_reginae "Strelitzia reginae")*)[[94]](#cite_note-Communications2003-96)
* the official plant is toyon (*[Heteromeles arbutifolia](/wiki/Heteromeles "Heteromeles")*)[[95]](#cite_note-97)

### Fauna

See also: [List of wild animals from Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_wild_animals_from_Los_Angeles "List of wild animals from Los Angeles")

The city has an urban [population](/wiki/Population_genetics "Population genetics") of [bobcats](/wiki/Bobcat "Bobcat") (*Lynx rufus*).[[96]](#cite_note-Serieys-et-al-2014-bundle-98) [Mange](/wiki/Mange "Mange") is a common problem in this population.[[96]](#cite_note-Serieys-et-al-2014-bundle-98) Although Serieys *et al.* 2014 find [selection](/wiki/Selection_(biology) "Selection (biology)") of [immune](/wiki/Immunity_(biology) "Immunity (biology)") genetics at several [loci](/wiki/Gene_locus "Gene locus") they do not demonstrate that [this produces](/wiki/Adaptation "Adaptation") a [real difference](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype") which helps the bobcats to [survive future mange outbreaks](/wiki/Disease_resistance "Disease resistance").[[96]](#cite_note-Serieys-et-al-2014-bundle-98)

Bats, coyotes, raccoons, opossums, skunks, snakes, and squirrels are wild animals commonly found in the Los Angeles area.[[97]](#cite_note-99)[[98]](#cite_note-100) [Mountain lions](/wiki/Cougar "Cougar"), [mule deer](/wiki/Mule_deer "Mule deer"), and other wild animals are also commonly spotted in Southern California.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

### Geology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Mt._Lukens_overlooking_La_Crescenta-Montrose.jpg/250px-Mt._Lukens_overlooking_La_Crescenta-Montrose.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mt._Lukens_overlooking_La_Crescenta-Montrose.jpg)

[Mount Lukens](/wiki/Mount_Lukens "Mount Lukens"), in the [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains "San Gabriel Mountains"), is the highest point in LA.

Los Angeles is subject to earthquakes because of its location on the [Pacific Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire"). The geologic instability has produced numerous [faults](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)"), which cause approximately 10,000 earthquakes annually in Southern California, though most of them are too small to be felt.[[100]](#cite_note-quakes-102) The [strike-slip](/wiki/Strike-slip_fault "Strike-slip fault") [San Andreas Fault](/wiki/San_Andreas_Fault "San Andreas Fault") system, which sits at the boundary between the [Pacific Plate](/wiki/Pacific_Plate "Pacific Plate") and the [North American Plate](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate"), passes through the Los Angeles metropolitan area. The segment of the fault passing through Southern California experiences a major earthquake roughly every 110 to 140 years, and [seismologists](/wiki/Seismologist "Seismologist") have warned about the next "big one", as the last major earthquake was the [1857 Fort Tejon earthquake](/wiki/1857_Fort_Tejon_earthquake "1857 Fort Tejon earthquake").[[101]](#cite_note-103) The Los Angeles basin and metropolitan area are also at risk from [blind thrust earthquakes](/wiki/Blind_thrust_earthquake "Blind thrust earthquake").[[102]](#cite_note-104) Major earthquakes that have hit the Los Angeles area include the [1933 Long Beach](/wiki/1933_Long_Beach_earthquake "1933 Long Beach earthquake"), [1971 San Fernando](/wiki/1971_San_Fernando_earthquake "1971 San Fernando earthquake"), [1987 Whittier Narrows](/wiki/1987_Whittier_Narrows_earthquake "1987 Whittier Narrows earthquake"), and the [1994 Northridge](/wiki/1994_Northridge_earthquake "1994 Northridge earthquake") events. All but a few are of low intensity and are not felt. The USGS has released the [UCERF California earthquake forecast](/wiki/California_earthquake_forecast "California earthquake forecast"), which models earthquake occurrence in California. Parts of the city are also vulnerable to [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami"); harbor areas were damaged by waves from [Aleutian Islands earthquake](/wiki/1946_Aleutian_Islands_earthquake "1946 Aleutian Islands earthquake") in 1946, [Valdivia earthquake](/wiki/1960_Valdivia_earthquake "1960 Valdivia earthquake") in 1960, [Alaska earthquake](/wiki/1964_Alaska_earthquake "1964 Alaska earthquake") in 1964, [Chile earthquake](/wiki/2010_Chile_earthquake "2010 Chile earthquake") in 2010 and [Japan earthquake](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami") in 2011.[[103]](#cite_note-105)

### Cityscape

Main article: [List of districts and neighborhoods in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_districts_and_neighborhoods_in_Los_Angeles "List of districts and neighborhoods in Los Angeles")

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Los_Angeles "List of tallest buildings in Los Angeles")

The city is divided into many different districts and neighborhoods,[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) some of which had been separately incorporated cities that eventually merged with Los Angeles.[[106]](#cite_note-Abu-Lughod1999-108) These neighborhoods were developed piecemeal, and are well-defined enough that the city has signage which marks nearly all of them.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

#### Overview

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/The_Griffith_Observatory_at_night%2C_Mt._Hollywood%2C_Los_Angeles%2C_California%2C_U.S.jpg/250px-The_Griffith_Observatory_at_night%2C_Mt._Hollywood%2C_Los_Angeles%2C_California%2C_U.S.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Griffith_Observatory_at_night,_Mt._Hollywood,_Los_Angeles,_California,_U.S.jpg)

View of the [downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles") skyline from [Griffith Park](/wiki/Griffith_Park "Griffith Park")

The city's street patterns generally follow a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), with uniform block lengths and occasional roads that cut across blocks. However, this is complicated by rugged terrain, which has necessitated having different grids for each of the valleys that Los Angeles covers. Major streets are designed to [move large volumes of traffic through many parts of the city](/w/index.php?title=Traffic_in_Los_Angeles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Traffic in Los Angeles (page does not exist)"), many of which are extremely long; [Sepulveda Boulevard](/wiki/Sepulveda_Boulevard "Sepulveda Boulevard") is 43 miles (69 km) long, while [Foothill Boulevard](/wiki/Foothill_Boulevard_(Southern_California) "Foothill Boulevard (Southern California)") is over 60 miles (97 km) long, reaching as far east as San Bernardino. Drivers in Los Angeles suffer from one of the worst rush hour periods in the world, according to an annual traffic index by navigation system maker, [TomTom](/wiki/TomTom "TomTom"). LA drivers spend an additional 92 hours in traffic each year. During the peak rush hour, there is 80% congestion, according to the index.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

Los Angeles is often characterized by the presence of [low-rise](/wiki/Low-rise "Low-rise") buildings, in contrast to New York City. Outside of a few centers such as [downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles"), [Warner Center](/wiki/Warner_Center "Warner Center"), [Century City](/wiki/Century_City "Century City"), [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Los_Angeles "Koreatown, Los Angeles"), [Miracle Mile](/wiki/Miracle_Mile,_Los_Angeles "Miracle Mile, Los Angeles"), Hollywood, and [Westwood](/wiki/Westwood,_Los_Angeles "Westwood, Los Angeles"), skyscrapers and high-rise buildings are not common in Los Angeles. However, downtown Los Angeles has many buildings over 30 stories, with fourteen over 50 stories, and two over 70 stories, the tallest of which is the [Wilshire Grand Center](/wiki/Wilshire_Grand_Center "Wilshire Grand Center").

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Los Angeles](/wiki/Climate_of_Los_Angeles "Climate of Los Angeles")

Los Angeles has a two-season [semi-arid climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *BSh*) with dry summers and very mild winters, but it receives more annual precipitation than most semi-arid climates, narrowly missing the boundary of a Mediterranean climate ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csb* on the coast, *Csa* otherwise).[[110]](#cite_note-Peel-112) Daytime temperatures are generally temperate all year round. In winter, they average around 68 °F (20 °C).[[111]](#cite_note-113) Autumn months tend to be hot, with major heat waves a common occurrence in September and October, while the spring months tend to be cooler and experience more precipitation. Los Angeles has plenty of sunshine throughout the year, with an average of only 35 days with measurable precipitation annually.[[112]](#cite_note-weatherbase-114)

Temperatures in the coastal basin exceed 90 °F (32 °C) on a dozen or so days in the year, from one day a month in April, May, June and November to three days a month in July, August, October and to five days in September.[[112]](#cite_note-weatherbase-114) Temperatures in the San Fernando and San Gabriel Valleys are considerably warmer. Temperatures are subject to substantial daily swings; in inland areas, the difference between the average daily low and the average daily high is over 30 °F (17 °C).[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115) The average annual temperature of the sea is 63 °F (17 °C), from 58 °F (14 °C) in January to 68 °F (20 °C) in August.[[114]](#cite_note-116) Hours of sunshine total more than 3,000 per year, from an average of 7 hours of sunshine per day in December to an average of 12 in July.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

Due to the mountainous terrain of the surrounding region, the Los Angeles area contains a large number of distinct [microclimates](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate"), causing extreme variations in temperature in close physical proximity to each other. For example, the average July maximum temperature at the [Santa Monica Pier](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Pier "Santa Monica Pier") is 70 °F (21 °C) whereas it is 95 °F (35 °C) in Canoga Park, 15 miles (24 km) away.[[116]](#cite_note-climateofCA-118) The city, like much of the Southern Californian coast, is subject to a late spring/early summer weather phenomenon called "[June Gloom](/wiki/June_Gloom "June Gloom")". This involves overcast or foggy skies in the morning that yield to sun by early afternoon.[[117]](#cite_note-Poole2010-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Lake_Hollywood_Reservoir_by_clinton_steeds.jpg/250px-Lake_Hollywood_Reservoir_by_clinton_steeds.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Hollywood_Reservoir_by_clinton_steeds.jpg)

[Lake Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_Reservoir "Hollywood Reservoir") in the [Santa Monica Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Monica_Mountains "Santa Monica Mountains")

More recently, statewide [droughts in California](/wiki/Droughts_in_California "Droughts in California") have further strained the city's [water security](/wiki/Water_security "Water security").[[118]](#cite_note-drought_continues-120) Downtown Los Angeles averages 14.67 in (373 mm) of precipitation annually, mainly occurring between November and March,[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115) generally in the form of moderate rain showers, but sometimes as heavy rainfall during winter storms. Rainfall is usually higher in the hills and coastal slopes of the mountains because of [orographic](/wiki/Orographic "Orographic") uplift. Summer days are usually rainless. Rarely, an incursion of moist air from the south or east can bring brief thunderstorms in late summer, especially to the mountains. The coast gets slightly less rainfall, while the inland and mountain areas get considerably more. Years of average rainfall are rare. The usual pattern is a year-to-year variability, with a short string of dry years of 5–10 in (130–250 mm) rainfall, followed by one or two wet years with more than 20 in (510 mm).[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115) Wet years are usually associated with warm water [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") conditions in the Pacific, dry years with cooler water [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña") episodes. A series of rainy days can bring floods to the lowlands and mudslides to the hills, especially after [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire") have denuded the slopes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Venice_Beach%2C_Los_Angeles%2C_CA_07.JPG/250px-Venice_Beach%2C_Los_Angeles%2C_CA_07.JPG)](/wiki/File:Venice_Beach,_Los_Angeles,_CA_07.JPG)

[Venice Beach](/wiki/Venice,_Los_Angeles "Venice, Los Angeles") on the [South Coast of California](/wiki/South_Coast_(California) "South Coast (California)")

Both freezing temperatures and snowfall are extremely rare in the city basin and along the coast, with the last occurrence of a 32 °F (0 °C) reading at the downtown station being January 29, 1979;[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115) freezing temperatures occur nearly every year in valley locations, while the mountains within city limits typically receive snowfall every winter. The greatest snowfall recorded in downtown Los Angeles was 2.0 inches (5 cm) on January 15, 1932.[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115)[[120]](#cite_note-BurtStroud2007-122) While the most recent snowfall occurred in February 2019, the first snowfall since 1962,[[121]](#cite_note-123)[[122]](#cite_note-124) with snow falling in areas adjacent to Los Angeles as recently as January 2021.[[123]](#cite_note-125) Brief, localized instances of hail can occur on rare occasions, but are more common than snowfall. At the official downtown station, the highest recorded temperature is 113 °F (45 °C) on September 27, 2010,[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115)[[124]](#cite_note-RecordHighTemp113-126) while the lowest is 28 °F (−2 °C),[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115) on January 4, 1949.[[113]](#cite_note-NCDC-CANOGA-115) Within the City of Los Angeles, the highest temperature ever officially recorded is 121 °F (49 °C), on September 6, 2020, at the weather station at [Pierce College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Pierce_College "Los Angeles Pierce College") in the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley") neighborhood of [Woodland Hills](/wiki/Woodland_Hills,_Los_Angeles "Woodland Hills, Los Angeles").[[125]](#cite_note-127) During autumn and winter, [Santa Ana winds](/wiki/Santa_Ana_winds "Santa Ana winds") sometimes bring much warmer and drier conditions to Los Angeles, and raise wildfire risk.

### Environmental issues

Further information: [Pollution in California § Los Angeles air pollution](/wiki/Pollution_in_California#Los_Angeles_air_pollution "Pollution in California")

| External audio | |
| --- | --- |
| [audio icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_arts.svg) ["Fighting Smog in Los Angeles"](https://www.sciencehistory.org/distillations/podcast/fighting-smog-in-los-angeles), *Distillations* Podcast, 2018 [Science History Institute](/wiki/Science_History_Institute "Science History Institute") | |

Owing to geography, heavy reliance on automobiles, and the Los Angeles/Long Beach port complex, Los Angeles suffers from air pollution in the form of smog. The [Los Angeles Basin](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Basin "Los Angeles Basin") and the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley") are susceptible to [atmospheric inversion](/wiki/Inversion_(meteorology) "Inversion (meteorology)"), which holds in the exhaust from road vehicles, airplanes, locomotives, shipping, manufacturing, and other sources.[[134]](#cite_note-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Los_Angeles_Pollution_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Pollution_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Pollution_(cropped).jpg)

Smog in Los Angeles in December 2005

The smog season lasts from approximately May to October.[[135]](#cite_note-Information1983-137) While other large cities rely on rain to clear smog, Los Angeles gets only 15 inches (380 mm) of rain each year: pollution accumulates over many consecutive days. Issues of air quality in Los Angeles and other major cities led to the passage of early national environmental legislation, including the [Clean Air Act](/wiki/United_States_Clean_Air_Act "United States Clean Air Act"). When the act was passed, California was unable to create a [State Implementation Plan](/wiki/State_Implementation_Plan "State Implementation Plan") that would enable it to meet the new air quality standards, largely because of the level of pollution in Los Angeles generated by older vehicles.[[136]](#cite_note-138) More recently, the state of California has led the nation in working to limit pollution by mandating [low-emission vehicles](/wiki/Low-emission_vehicle "Low-emission vehicle"). Smog is expected to continue to drop in the coming years because of aggressive steps to reduce it, which include [electric](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car") and [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle") cars, improvements in [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit"), and other measures.

The number of Stage 1 smog alerts in Los Angeles has declined from over 100 per year in the 1970s to almost zero in the new millennium.[[137]](#cite_note-139) Despite improvement, the 2006 and 2007 annual reports of the [American Lung Association](/wiki/American_Lung_Association "American Lung Association") ranked the city as the most polluted in the country with short-term particle pollution and year-round particle pollution.[[138]](#cite_note-shortTermPollutedCities-140) In 2008, the city was ranked the second most polluted and again had the highest year-round particulate pollution.[[139]](#cite_note-141) The city met its goal of providing 20 percent of the city's power from renewable sources in 2010.[[140]](#cite_note-Renewable_Energy-142) The American Lung Association's 2013 survey ranks the metro area as having the nation's worst smog, and fourth in both short-term and year-round pollution amounts.[[141]](#cite_note-143)

Los Angeles is also home to the nation's largest urban [oil field](/wiki/Oil_field "Oil field"). There are more than 700 active oil wells within 1,500 feet (460 m) of homes, churches, schools, and hospitals in the city, a situation about which the [EPA](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") has voiced serious concerns.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

### Total population, age, and sex

The [2020 U.S. census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") reported Los Angeles had a population of 3,898,747.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) The population density was 8,304.2 people per square mile (3,168 people per square kilometer). 5.2% of the total population is under 5 years old, 19.5% under 18 and 13.8% 65 years old and over.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) Females make up 50.2% of the total population.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162)

### Housing and families

Owner-occupied housing units make up 36.3% of the total Los Angeles housing units, and they cost $879,500 on average. (2019–2023)[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) With a mortgage, the median selected monthly owner costs are $3,399, and without a mortgage $950. (2019–2023)[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) Median gross rent is $1,879. (2019–2023)[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) There are 1,419,663 households in Los Angeles, with an average of 2.64 people being part of them. (2019–2023).[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162)

### Languages

Well over 200 languages are spoken in Los Angeles. The 2021 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") of the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") reported that some 56.8% of city residents aged five and older spoke a language other than English at home.[[161]](#cite_note-163)

### Economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Distribution_of_high_income_households_across_LA_County.png/250px-Distribution_of_high_income_households_across_LA_County.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_high_income_households_across_LA_County.png)

Percentage of households with incomes above $150k across [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California") census tracts

Around 66.5% of the total population aged 16 and over make up Los Angeles in civilian labor force, while among female residents aged 16 and over the percentage is 61.0%.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) In 2022, accommodation and food services made $17,366,966, health care and social assistance sectors made $46,297,839, transportation and warehousing $25,410,257, and the retail sector $81,351,523, with residents spending an average of $21,281 in retail purchases throughout the year.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) From 2019 to 2023, the median household income in Los Angeles was $80,366 (2023 dollars), while the per capita income in the past 12 months was $46,270.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162) Some 16.5% of Los Angeles residents have total incomes below the federal poverty line.[[160]](#cite_note-:2-162)

### Race and ethnicity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Los_angeles_chinatown0001.jpg/250px-Los_angeles_chinatown0001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_angeles_chinatown0001.jpg)

Los Angeles [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Los_Angeles "Chinatown, Los Angeles")

According to data in 2023 from the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), Los Angeles's population is 47.2% [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), 28.3% non-Hispanic [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 8.5% [Black](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 12.0% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 1.2% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and 0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans").[[162]](#cite_note-164) [Ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") like [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Los_Angeles "Chinatown, Los Angeles"), [Historic Filipinotown](/wiki/Historic_Filipinotown,_Los_Angeles "Historic Filipinotown, Los Angeles"), [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Los_Angeles "Koreatown, Los Angeles"), [Little Armenia](/wiki/Little_Armenia,_Los_Angeles "Little Armenia, Los Angeles"), [Little Ethiopia](/wiki/Little_Ethiopia,_Los_Angeles "Little Ethiopia, Los Angeles"), [Tehrangeles](/wiki/Tehrangeles "Tehrangeles"), [Little Tokyo](/wiki/Little_Tokyo,_Los_Angeles "Little Tokyo, Los Angeles"), [Little Bangladesh](/wiki/Little_Bangladesh,_Los_Angeles "Little Bangladesh, Los Angeles"), and [Thai Town](/wiki/Thai_Town,_Los_Angeles "Thai Town, Los Angeles") provide examples of the [polyglot](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") character of Los Angeles.
[Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") ancestry makes up the largest origin among descendants of [American countries](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_in_the_Americas "List of sovereign states and dependent territories in the Americas") other than the United States at 31.9% of the city's population, followed by those of [Salvadoran](/wiki/Salvadoran_American "Salvadoran American") (6.0%) and [Guatemalan](/wiki/Guatemalan_American "Guatemalan American") (3.6%) heritage. Descendants of Mexicans and Central Americans have long-established communities in Los Angeles and are spread throughout the entire city and its metropolitan area. It is most heavily concentrated in regions around Downtown, such as [East Los Angeles](/wiki/East_Los_Angeles_(region) "East Los Angeles (region)"), [Northeast Los Angeles](/wiki/Northeast_Los_Angeles "Northeast Los Angeles") and [Westlake](/wiki/Westlake,_Los_Angeles "Westlake, Los Angeles").[[163]](#cite_note-165)

The largest Asian ethnic groups are [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipino_American "Filipino American") (3.2%) and [Koreans](/wiki/Korean_American "Korean American") (2.9%), which have their own established ethnic enclaves—[Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Los_Angeles "Koreatown, Los Angeles") in the Wilshire Center and [Historic Filipinotown](/wiki/Historic_Filipinotown "Historic Filipinotown").[[164]](#cite_note-166) [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American") people, which make up 1.8% of Los Angeles's population, reside mostly outside of Los Angeles city limits, in the [San Gabriel Valley](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Valley "San Gabriel Valley") of eastern Los Angeles County, but make a sizable presence in the city, notably in [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Los_Angeles "Chinatown, Los Angeles").[[165]](#cite_note-167) Chinatown and [Thaitown](/wiki/Thaitown,_Los_Angeles,_California "Thaitown, Los Angeles, California") are also home to many [Thais](/wiki/Thai_American "Thai American") and [Cambodians](/wiki/Cambodian_American "Cambodian American"), which make up 0.3% and 0.1% of Los Angeles's population, respectively. The [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_American "Japanese American") comprise 0.9% of the city's population and have an established [Little Tokyo](/wiki/Little_Tokyo,_Los_Angeles "Little Tokyo, Los Angeles") in the city's downtown, and another significant community of Japanese Americans is in the [Sawtelle](/wiki/Sawtelle,_Los_Angeles "Sawtelle, Los Angeles") district of West Los Angeles. [Indians](/wiki/Indian_American "Indian American") make up 0.9% of the city's population. [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_American "Vietnamese American") make up 0.5% of Los Angeles's population.

Los Angeles is also home to Caucasian and Middle Eastern communities, such as [Armenians](/wiki/Armenian_American "Armenian American"), [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_Americans "Assyrian Americans"), and [Iranians](/wiki/Iranian_Americans "Iranian Americans"), many of whom live in enclaves like [Little Armenia](/wiki/Little_Armenia,_Los_Angeles "Little Armenia, Los Angeles") and [Tehrangeles](/wiki/Tehrangeles "Tehrangeles").[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)

African Americans have been the predominant ethnic group in [South Los Angeles](/wiki/South_Los_Angeles "South Los Angeles"), which has emerged as the largest African-American community in the western United States since the 1960s. The neighborhoods of South Los Angeles with highest concentration of African Americans include [Crenshaw](/wiki/Crenshaw,_Los_Angeles "Crenshaw, Los Angeles"), [Baldwin Hills](/wiki/Baldwin_Hills,_Los_Angeles "Baldwin Hills, Los Angeles"), [Leimert Park](/wiki/Leimert_Park,_Los_Angeles "Leimert Park, Los Angeles"), [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Los_Angeles "Hyde Park, Los Angeles"), [Gramercy Park](/wiki/Gramercy_Park,_Los_Angeles "Gramercy Park, Los Angeles"), [Manchester Square](/wiki/Manchester_Square,_Los_Angeles "Manchester Square, Los Angeles") and [Watts](/wiki/Watts,_Los_Angeles "Watts, Los Angeles").[[168]](#cite_note-MaryEllen-170) Since the 1990s, the growing cost of living in the city has most impacted the African American population. African Americans are the fastest declining population in the city, and many of the formerly predominantly African American neighborhoods have become much more diverse.[[169]](#cite_note-171)[[170]](#cite_note-172) There is also a sizable [Eritrean](/wiki/Eritrean_Americans "Eritrean Americans") and [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopian_Americans "Ethiopian Americans") community in the Fairfax region.[[171]](#cite_note-173)

Los Angeles has the second-largest Mexican, Armenian, Salvadoran, Filipino, and Guatemalan populations by city in the world, the third-largest Canadian population in the world, and has the largest Japanese, Iranian/Persian, Cambodian, and Romani (Gypsy) populations in the country.[[172]](#cite_note-174) The Italian community is concentrated in San Pedro.[[173]](#cite_note-175)

Most of Los Angeles's foreign-born population were born in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[174]](#cite_note-176)
