According to a 2014 study by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the most prevalently practiced religion in Los Angeles (65%).[[175]](#cite_note-LAReligion1-177)[[176]](#cite_note-LAReligion2-178) The [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Los Angeles](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Los_Angeles "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Los Angeles") is the largest [archdiocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") in the country.[[177]](#cite_note-179) [Cardinal Roger Mahony](/wiki/Roger_Mahony "Roger Mahony"), as the archbishop, oversaw construction of the [Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels "Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels"), which opened in September 2002 in downtown Los Angeles.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

In 2011, the once common, but ultimately lapsed, custom of conducting a procession and [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") in honor of Nuestra Señora de los Ángeles, in commemoration of the founding of the City of Los Angeles in 1781, was revived by the [Queen of Angels Foundation](/wiki/Queen_of_Angels_Foundation "Queen of Angels Foundation") and its founder Mark Albert, with the support of the Archdiocese of Los Angeles as well as several civic leaders.[[179]](#cite_note-181) The recently revived custom is a continuation of the original processions and Masses that commenced on the first anniversary of the founding of Los Angeles in 1782 and continued for nearly a century thereafter.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Exterior_of_Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels_dllu.jpg/250px-Exterior_of_Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels_dllu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels_dllu.jpg)

[Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels "Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels") (also called Los Angeles Cathedral), the metropolitan cathedral of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") in Los Angeles.

With 621,000 [Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") in the metropolitan area, the region has the second-largest population of Jews in the United States, after [New York City](/wiki/Judaism_in_New_York_City "Judaism in New York City").[[180]](#cite_note-Jews-182) Many of Los Angeles's Jews now live on the [Westside](/wiki/Westside_(Los_Angeles_County) "Westside (Los Angeles County)") and in the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley"), though [Boyle Heights](/wiki/Boyle_Heights,_Los_Angeles,_California "Boyle Heights, Los Angeles, California") once had a large Jewish population before World War II due to [restrictive housing covenants](/wiki/Covenant_(law)#Exclusionary_covenants "Covenant (law)"). Major Orthodox Jewish neighborhoods include [Hancock Park](/wiki/Hancock_Park,_Los_Angeles "Hancock Park, Los Angeles"), [Pico-Robertson](/wiki/Pico-Robertson "Pico-Robertson"), and [Valley Village](/wiki/Valley_Village,_Los_Angeles "Valley Village, Los Angeles"), while Jewish Israelis are well represented in the [Encino](/wiki/Encino,_Los_Angeles "Encino, Los Angeles") and [Tarzana](/wiki/Tarzana,_Los_Angeles "Tarzana, Los Angeles") neighborhoods, and [Persian Jews](/wiki/Persian_Jews "Persian Jews") in [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_California "Beverly Hills, California"). Many varieties of Judaism are represented in the greater Los Angeles area, including [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism"), [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Judaism "Conservative Judaism"), [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism"), and [Reconstructionist](/wiki/Reconstructionist_Judaism "Reconstructionist Judaism"). The [Breed Street Shul](/wiki/Breed_Street_Shul "Breed Street Shul") in [East Los Angeles](/wiki/East_Los_Angeles,_California "East Los Angeles, California"), built in 1923, was the largest synagogue west of Chicago in its early decades; it is no longer in daily use as a synagogue and is being converted to a museum and community center.[[181]](#cite_note-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184) The [Kabbalah Centre](/wiki/Kabbalah_Centre "Kabbalah Centre") also has a presence in the city.[[183]](#cite_note-185)

The [International Church of the Foursquare Gospel](/wiki/International_Church_of_the_Foursquare_Gospel "International Church of the Foursquare Gospel") was founded in Los Angeles by [Aimee Semple McPherson](/wiki/Aimee_Semple_McPherson "Aimee Semple McPherson") in 1923 and remains headquartered there to this day. For many years, the church convened at [Angelus Temple](/wiki/Angelus_Temple "Angelus Temple"), which, at its construction, was one of the largest churches in the country.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Wilshire_Boulevard_Temple_2017_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Wilshire_Boulevard_Temple_2017_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilshire_Boulevard_Temple_2017_(cropped).jpg)

[Wilshire Boulevard Temple](/wiki/Wilshire_Boulevard_Temple "Wilshire Boulevard Temple") is one of the largest [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") in LA.

Los Angeles has had a rich and influential Protestant tradition. The first Protestant service in Los Angeles was a Methodist meeting held in a private home in 1850, and the oldest Protestant church still operating, [First Congregational Church](/wiki/First_Congregational_Church_of_Los_Angeles "First Congregational Church of Los Angeles"), was founded in 1867.[[185]](#cite_note-Eighteen5019Thirty-187) In the early 1900s the [Bible Institute Of Los Angeles](/wiki/Bible_Institute_Of_Los_Angeles "Bible Institute Of Los Angeles") published the founding documents of the [Christian Fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_Fundamentalist "Christian Fundamentalist") movement and the [Azusa Street Revival](/wiki/Azusa_Street_Revival "Azusa Street Revival") launched [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism").[[185]](#cite_note-Eighteen5019Thirty-187) The [Metropolitan Community Church](/wiki/Metropolitan_Community_Church "Metropolitan Community Church") also had its origins in the Los Angeles area.[[186]](#cite_note-188) Important churches in the city include [First Presbyterian Church of Hollywood](/wiki/First_Presbyterian_Church_of_Hollywood "First Presbyterian Church of Hollywood"), [Bel Air Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Bel_Air_Presbyterian_Church "Bel Air Presbyterian Church"), [First African Methodist Episcopal Church of Los Angeles](/wiki/First_African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church_of_Los_Angeles "First African Methodist Episcopal Church of Los Angeles"), [West Angeles Church of God in Christ](/wiki/West_Angeles_Church_of_God_in_Christ "West Angeles Church of God in Christ"), [Second Baptist Church](/wiki/Second_Baptist_Church_(Los_Angeles,_California) "Second Baptist Church (Los Angeles, California)"), [Crenshaw Christian Center](/wiki/Crenshaw_Christian_Center "Crenshaw Christian Center"), [McCarty Memorial Christian Church](/wiki/McCarty_Memorial_Christian_Church "McCarty Memorial Christian Church"), and First Congregational Church.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Second_Church_of_Christ%2C_Scientist%2C_Los_Angeles.jpg/250px-Second_Church_of_Christ%2C_Scientist%2C_Los_Angeles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_Church_of_Christ,_Scientist,_Los_Angeles.jpg)

[Second Church of Christ, Scientist](/wiki/Second_Church_of_Christ,_Scientist_(Los_Angeles) "Second Church of Christ, Scientist (Los Angeles)")

The Hollywood region of Los Angeles also has several significant headquarters, churches, including the [Celebrity Center](/wiki/Celebrity_Center "Celebrity Center") of the [Church of Scientology](/wiki/Church_of_Scientology "Church of Scientology").[[187]](#cite_note-189)

Because of Los Angeles's large multi-ethnic population, a wide variety of faiths are practiced, including [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Los_Angeles "Hinduism in Los Angeles"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), various [Eastern Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Sufism](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism"), [Shintoism](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") and countless others. Immigrants from Asia, for example, have formed several significant [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") congregations, making the city home to the greatest variety of Buddhists in the world. The first Buddhist [joss house](/wiki/Chinese_temple_architecture "Chinese temple architecture") was founded in the city in 1875.[[185]](#cite_note-Eighteen5019Thirty-187) [Atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") and other secular beliefs are also common, as the city is the largest in the Western U.S. [Unchurched Belt](/wiki/Unchurched_Belt "Unchurched Belt").

### Homelessness

Main article: [Homelessness in Los Angeles](/wiki/Homelessness_in_Los_Angeles "Homelessness in Los Angeles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Homeless_people%2C_Los_Angeles%2C_California_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Homeless_people%2C_Los_Angeles%2C_California_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Homeless_people,_Los_Angeles,_California_(cropped).jpg)

Homeless tents outside [Los Angeles City Hall](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Hall "Los Angeles City Hall"), 2021

As of January 2024, there are 45,252 [homeless people](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_United_States "Homelessness in the United States") in the City of Los Angeles, comprising roughly 60% of the homeless population of LA County.[[188]](#cite_note-190) This is a 2.2% decrease from the previous year (with a 0.3% decrease in the overall homeless population of LA County).[[189]](#cite_note-:0-191)[[190]](#cite_note-Homeless_Populations-192) The epicenter of homelessness in Los Angeles is the [Skid Row](/wiki/Skid_Row,_Los_Angeles "Skid Row, Los Angeles") neighborhood, which contains 8,000 homeless people, one of the largest stable populations of homeless people in the United States.[[191]](#cite_note-193)[[192]](#cite_note-abc7_06132019-194) The increased homeless population in Los Angeles has been attributed to lack of housing affordability[[190]](#cite_note-Homeless_Populations-192) and to substance abuse.[[193]](#cite_note-195) Almost 60 percent of the 82,955 people who became newly homeless in 2019 said their homelessness was because of economic hardship.[[189]](#cite_note-:0-191) In Los Angeles, black people are roughly four times more likely to experience homelessness.[[189]](#cite_note-:0-191)[[194]](#cite_note-196)

## Economy

Further information: [Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chamber_of_Commerce "Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce")

See also: [Los Angeles County, California § Economy](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California#Economy "Los Angeles County, California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Tree_Map_of_Employment_by_Industries_in_Los_Angeles_County%2C_Ca_%282015%29.svg/250px-Tree_Map_of_Employment_by_Industries_in_Los_Angeles_County%2C_Ca_%282015%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tree_Map_of_Employment_by_Industries_in_Los_Angeles_County,_Ca_(2015).svg)

Employment by industry in [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California") in 2015

The economy of Los Angeles is driven by international trade, entertainment (television, motion pictures, video games, music recording, and production), aerospace, technology, petroleum, fashion, apparel, and tourism.[[195]](#cite_note-197) Other significant industries include finance, telecommunications, law, healthcare, and [transportation](/wiki/Transportation_in_Los_Angeles "Transportation in Los Angeles"). In the 2017 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"), Los Angeles was ranked the 19th most competitive financial center in the world and sixth most competitive in the U.S. after [New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City#Wall_Street "Economy of New York City"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco#Economy "San Francisco"), [Chicago](/wiki/Economy_of_Chicago#Finance "Economy of Chicago"), [Boston](/wiki/Economy_of_Boston "Economy of Boston"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Economy_of_Washington,_D.C. "Economy of Washington, D.C.")[[196]](#cite_note-198) Although many businesses have left [downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles") following the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), efforts are underway to re-invent the neighborhood as a cultural center with a large architectural showcase in [Bunker Hill](/wiki/Bunker_Hill,_Los_Angeles "Bunker Hill, Los Angeles") designed by [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry").[[24]](#cite_note-DowntownLosAngelesHurting-26)

Of the five [major film studios](/wiki/Major_film_studio "Major film studio"), only [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") is within Los Angeles's city limits;[[197]](#cite_note-199) it is located in the so-called [Thirty-Mile Zone](/wiki/Studio_zone "Studio zone") of entertainment headquarters in Southern California. Their parent company [Paramount Skydance Corporation](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance") has had its corporate headquarters in Los Angeles since 2025.[[198]](#cite_note-HQ-200)

Los Angeles is still the largest manufacturing center in the United States by employment, but now second after the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") by dollar value of goods manufactured. Disadvantages of manufacturing production in Los Angeles are its relative geographical isolation from most major North American markets, lack of a reliable water supply, and lack of an adequate skilled labor force, in addition to a significantly complex regulatory environment.[[199]](#cite_note-201) The contiguous [ports of Los Angeles](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles") and [Long Beach](/wiki/Port_of_Long_Beach "Port of Long Beach") together comprise the busiest port in the United States by some measures and the fifth busiest port in the world, vital to trade within the [Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim "Pacific Rim").[[200]](#cite_note-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Los_Angeles_Harbor_-_panoramio_-_Zzyzx_%281%29.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Harbor_-_panoramio_-_Zzyzx_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Harbor_-_panoramio_-_Zzyzx_(1).jpg)

The combined [Port of Los Angeles](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles")-[Port of Long Beach](/wiki/Port_of_Long_Beach "Port of Long Beach") is the fifth-busiest port in the world.

The [Los Angeles metropolitan area](/wiki/Los_Angeles_metropolitan_area "Los Angeles metropolitan area") has a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") of over $1.0 trillion (as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles&action=edit)),[[201]](#cite_note-bea-203) making it the third-largest economic metropolitan area in the world, after [New York](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") and [Tokyo](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area").[[201]](#cite_note-bea-203) Los Angeles has been classified an "[alpha world city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city")" according to a 2012 study by a group at [Loughborough University](/wiki/Loughborough_University "Loughborough University").[[202]](#cite_note-204)

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Los_Angeles&action=edit), Los Angeles is home to three [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies: [AECOM](/wiki/AECOM "AECOM"), [CBRE Group](/wiki/CBRE_Group "CBRE Group"), and [Reliance Steel & Aluminum Co](/wiki/Reliance_Steel_%26_Aluminum_Co. "Reliance Steel & Aluminum Co.").[[203]](#cite_note-Fortune_500-205) Other companies headquartered in Los Angeles and the surrounding metropolitan area include [The Aerospace Corporation](/wiki/The_Aerospace_Corporation "The Aerospace Corporation"), [California Pizza Kitchen](/wiki/California_Pizza_Kitchen "California Pizza Kitchen"),[[204]](#cite_note-206) [Capital Group Companies](/wiki/Capital_Group_Companies "Capital Group Companies"), [Deluxe Entertainment Services Group](/wiki/Deluxe_Entertainment_Services_Group "Deluxe Entertainment Services Group"), [Dine Brands Global](/wiki/Dine_Brands_Global "Dine Brands Global"), [DreamWorks Animation](/wiki/DreamWorks_Animation "DreamWorks Animation"), [Dollar Shave Club](/wiki/Dollar_Shave_Club "Dollar Shave Club"), [Fandango Media](/wiki/Fandango_Media "Fandango Media"), [Farmers Insurance Group](/wiki/Farmers_Insurance_Group "Farmers Insurance Group"), [Forever 21](/wiki/Forever_21 "Forever 21"), [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"), [Panda Express](/wiki/Panda_Express "Panda Express"), [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX"), [Ubisoft Film & Television](/wiki/Ubisoft_Film_%26_Television "Ubisoft Film & Television"), [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros."), [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group"), and [Trader Joe's](/wiki/Trader_Joe%27s "Trader Joe's").

At the end of the second quarter of 2024, Los Angeles saw an office space vacancy rate of 31.5%, a 33.5% increase year-over-year[[205]](#cite_note-auto-207)[[206]](#cite_note-:1-208) Retail vacancy stood at 8.6%, a 15% increase year-over-year.[[206]](#cite_note-:1-208)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Skyline_view_of_Los_Angeles%2C_California_LCCN2013631685_%28cropped%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Skyline_view_of_Los_Angeles%2C_California_LCCN2013631685_%28cropped%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skyline_view_of_Los_Angeles,_California_LCCN2013631685_(cropped).tif)

[Downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles") is the [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") of the city.

## Arts and culture

Main article: [Culture of Los Angeles](/wiki/Culture_of_Los_Angeles "Culture of Los Angeles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Los_Angeles_Plaza-1.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Plaza-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Plaza-1.jpg)

The city's historic center at [Plaza de Los Ángeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Plaza "Los Angeles Plaza") near [Calle Olvera](/wiki/Olvera_Street "Olvera Street")

Los Angeles is often billed as the creative capital of the world because one in every six of its residents works in a creative industry[[208]](#cite_note-210) and there are more artists, writers, filmmakers, actors, dancers and musicians living and working in Los Angeles than any other city at any other time in [world history](/wiki/Human_history "Human history").[[209]](#cite_note-Only_In_LA-211) Los Angeles is strongly influenced by Mexican American culture due to California formerly being part of Mexico and, previously, the Spanish Empire.[[210]](#cite_note-212) The city is also known for [its prolific murals](/wiki/Murals_of_Los_Angeles "Murals of Los Angeles").[[211]](#cite_note-shatkin2013-213)

### Landmarks

See also: [List of sites of interest in the Los Angeles area](/wiki/List_of_sites_of_interest_in_the_Los_Angeles_area "List of sites of interest in the Los Angeles area") and [National Register of Historic Places listings in Los Angeles, California](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Los_Angeles,_California "National Register of Historic Places listings in Los Angeles, California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/El_Cabrillo_Courtyard.png/250px-El_Cabrillo_Courtyard.png)](/wiki/File:El_Cabrillo_Courtyard.png)

[El Cabrillo](/wiki/El_Cabrillo "El Cabrillo"), a [Spanish Revival](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival "Spanish Colonial Revival") style [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark")

The [architecture of Los Angeles](/wiki/Culture_of_Los_Angeles#Architecture "Culture of Los Angeles") is influenced by its Spanish, Mexican, and American roots. Popular styles in the city include [Spanish Colonial Revival](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival "Spanish Colonial Revival") style, [Mission Revival](/wiki/Mission_Revival "Mission Revival") style, [California Churrigueresque](/wiki/California_Churrigueresque "California Churrigueresque") style, [Mediterranean Revival](/wiki/Mediterranean_Revival "Mediterranean Revival") style, [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco_architecture "Art Deco architecture") style, and [Mid-Century Modern](/wiki/Mid-century_modern "Mid-century modern") style, among others.

Important landmarks in Los Angeles include the [Hollywood Sign](/wiki/Hollywood_Sign "Hollywood Sign"),[[212]](#cite_note-214) [Walt Disney Concert Hall](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Concert_Hall "Walt Disney Concert Hall"), [Capitol Records Building](/wiki/Capitol_Records_Building "Capitol Records Building"),[[213]](#cite_note-215) the [Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Our_Lady_of_the_Angels "Cathedral of Our Lady of the Angels"),[[214]](#cite_note-216) [Angels Flight](/wiki/Angels_Flight "Angels Flight"),[[215]](#cite_note-217) [Grauman's Chinese Theatre](/wiki/Grauman%27s_Chinese_Theatre "Grauman's Chinese Theatre"),[[216]](#cite_note-218) [Dolby Theatre](/wiki/Dolby_Theatre "Dolby Theatre"),[[217]](#cite_note-219) [Griffith Observatory](/wiki/Griffith_Observatory "Griffith Observatory"),[[218]](#cite_note-220) [Getty Center](/wiki/Getty_Center "Getty Center"),[[219]](#cite_note-221) [Getty Villa](/wiki/Getty_Villa "Getty Villa"),[[220]](#cite_note-222) [Stahl House](/wiki/Stahl_House "Stahl House"),[[221]](#cite_note-223) the [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum"), [L.A. Live](/wiki/L.A._Live "L.A. Live"),[[222]](#cite_note-224) the [Los Angeles County Museum of Art](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Museum_of_Art "Los Angeles County Museum of Art"), the [Venice Canal Historic District](/wiki/Venice_Canal_Historic_District "Venice Canal Historic District") and boardwalk, [Theme Building](/wiki/Theme_Building "Theme Building"), [Bradbury Building](/wiki/Bradbury_Building "Bradbury Building"), [U.S. Bank Tower](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Tower "U.S. Bank Tower"), [Wilshire Grand Center](/wiki/Wilshire_Grand_Center "Wilshire Grand Center"), [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard"), [Los Angeles City Hall](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Hall "Los Angeles City Hall"), [Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl"),[[223]](#cite_note-225) battleship [USS *Iowa*](/wiki/USS_Iowa_(BB-61) "USS Iowa (BB-61)"), [Watts Towers](/wiki/Watts_Towers "Watts Towers"),[[224]](#cite_note-226) [Crypto.com Arena](/wiki/Crypto.com_Arena "Crypto.com Arena"), [Dodger Stadium](/wiki/Dodger_Stadium "Dodger Stadium"), and [Olvera Street](/wiki/Olvera_Street "Olvera Street").[[225]](#cite_note-227)

### Movies and the performing arts

Further information: [Music of Los Angeles](/wiki/Music_of_Los_Angeles "Music of Los Angeles")

See also: [List of films set in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_films_set_in_Los_Angeles "List of films set in Los Angeles") and [List of songs about Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_songs_about_Los_Angeles "List of songs about Los Angeles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Chinese_Theatre_%2826776735090%29.jpg/250px-Chinese_Theatre_%2826776735090%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinese_Theatre_(26776735090).jpg)

[Grauman's Chinese Theatre](/wiki/Grauman%27s_Chinese_Theatre "Grauman's Chinese Theatre") on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame")

The performing arts play a major role in Los Angeles's cultural identity. According to the USC Stevens Institute for Innovation, "there are more than 1,100 annual theatrical productions and 21 openings every week."[[209]](#cite_note-Only_In_LA-211) The [Los Angeles Music Center](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Music_Center "Los Angeles Music Center") is "one of the three largest performing arts centers in the nation", with more than 1.3 million visitors per year.[[226]](#cite_note-228) The [Walt Disney Concert Hall](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Concert_Hall "Walt Disney Concert Hall"), centerpiece of the Music Center, is home to the prestigious [Los Angeles Philharmonic](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Philharmonic "Los Angeles Philharmonic").[[227]](#cite_note-229) Notable organizations such as [Center Theatre Group](/wiki/Center_Theatre_Group "Center Theatre Group"), the [Los Angeles Master Chorale](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Master_Chorale "Los Angeles Master Chorale"), and the [Los Angeles Opera](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Opera "Los Angeles Opera") are also resident companies of the Music Center.[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232) Talent is locally cultivated at premier institutions such as the [Colburn School](/wiki/Colburn_School "Colburn School") and the [USC Thornton School of Music](/wiki/USC_Thornton_School_of_Music "USC Thornton School of Music").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Hollywood_bowl_and_sign.jpg/250px-Hollywood_bowl_and_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollywood_bowl_and_sign.jpg)

[Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl") in [Hollywood Hills](/wiki/Hollywood_Hills "Hollywood Hills")

The city's [Hollywood neighborhood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") has been recognized as the center of the [motion picture industry](/wiki/Film_industry "Film industry"), having held this distinction since the early 20th century, and the Los Angeles area is also associated with being the center of the [television industry](/wiki/Television_industry "Television industry").[[231]](#cite_note-233) The city is home to major film studios as well as major record labels. Los Angeles plays host to the annual [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), the [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"), the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), as well as many other entertainment industry awards shows. Los Angeles is the site of the [USC School of Cinematic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts") which is the oldest film school in the United States.[[232]](#cite_note-234)

### Museums and galleries

See also: [List of museums in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Los_Angeles "List of museums in Los Angeles") and [List of museums in Los Angeles County, California](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Los_Angeles_County,_California "List of museums in Los Angeles County, California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Getty_Villa_exterior.jpg/250px-Getty_Villa_exterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Getty_Villa_exterior.jpg)

The [Getty Villa](/wiki/Getty_Villa "Getty Villa") is one of the two campuses of the [J. Paul Getty Museum](/wiki/J._Paul_Getty_Museum "J. Paul Getty Museum"), alongside the [Getty Center](/wiki/Getty_Center "Getty Center").

There are 841 museums and art galleries in [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California"), including many in the city of Los Angeles,[[233]](#cite_note-lmu-235) more museums per capita than any other city in the U.S.[[233]](#cite_note-lmu-235) Some of the notable museums are the [Los Angeles County Museum of Art](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Museum_of_Art "Los Angeles County Museum of Art") (the largest art museum in the Western United States[[234]](#cite_note-236)), the [Getty Center](/wiki/Getty_Center "Getty Center") (part of the [J. Paul Getty Trust](/wiki/J._Paul_Getty_Trust "J. Paul Getty Trust"), the world's wealthiest art institution[[235]](#cite_note-237)), the [Petersen Automotive Museum](/wiki/Petersen_Automotive_Museum "Petersen Automotive Museum"),[[236]](#cite_note-238) the [Huntington Library](/wiki/Huntington_Library "Huntington Library"),[[237]](#cite_note-239) the [Natural History Museum](/wiki/Natural_History_Museum_of_Los_Angeles_County "Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County"),[[238]](#cite_note-240) the [Battleship Iowa](/wiki/Battleship_Iowa "Battleship Iowa"),[[239]](#cite_note-241) [The Broad](/wiki/The_Broad "The Broad"), which houses over 2,000 works of contemporary art[[240]](#cite_note-242) and the [Museum of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art,_Los_Angeles "Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles").[[241]](#cite_note-243) A significant number of art galleries are on [Gallery Row](/wiki/Gallery_Row "Gallery Row"), and tens of thousands attend the monthly Downtown Art Walk there.[[242]](#cite_note-244)

### Libraries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Los_Angeles_Central_Library%2C_630_W._5th_St._Downtown_Los_Angeles_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Central_Library%2C_630_W._5th_St._Downtown_Los_Angeles_2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Central_Library,_630_W._5th_St._Downtown_Los_Angeles_2_(cropped).jpg)

[Los Angeles Central Library](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Public_Library "Los Angeles Public Library")

The [Los Angeles Public Library](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Public_Library "Los Angeles Public Library") system operates 72 public libraries in the city.[[243]](#cite_note-245) Enclaves of unincorporated areas are served by branches of the [County of Los Angeles Public Library](/wiki/County_of_Los_Angeles_Public_Library "County of Los Angeles Public Library"), many of which are within walking distance to residents.[[244]](#cite_note-246)

### Cuisine

See also: [List of Michelin-starred restaurants in California](/wiki/List_of_Michelin-starred_restaurants_in_California "List of Michelin-starred restaurants in California")

Los Angeles's food culture is a fusion of global cuisine brought on by the city's rich immigrant history and population. As of 2025, the [Michelin](/wiki/Michelin "Michelin") Guide recognized 20 starred restaurants including 2 restaurants [Providence](/wiki/Providence_(restaurant) "Providence (restaurant)") and [Somni](/wiki/Somni "Somni") which earned 3 Michelin stars.[[245]](#cite_note-247)

Latin American immigrants, particularly [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_cuisine "Mexican cuisine") immigrants, brought [tacos](/wiki/Taco "Taco"), [burritos](/wiki/Burrito "Burrito"), [quesadillas](/wiki/Quesadilla "Quesadilla"), [tortas](/wiki/Torta "Torta"), [tamales](/wiki/Tamale "Tamale"), and [enchiladas](/wiki/Enchilada "Enchilada") served from food trucks and stands, [taquerias](/wiki/Taqueria "Taqueria"), and [cafés](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9 "Café"). Asian restaurants, many immigrant-owned, exist throughout the city with hotspots in [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown "Chinatown"),[[246]](#cite_note-248) [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Los_Angeles "Koreatown, Los Angeles"),[[247]](#cite_note-249) and [Little Tokyo](/wiki/Little_Tokyo,_Los_Angeles "Little Tokyo, Los Angeles").[[248]](#cite_note-250) Los Angeles also carries an outsized offering of vegan, vegetarian, and plant-based options.

## Sports

See also: [Soccer in Los Angeles](/wiki/Soccer_in_Los_Angeles "Soccer in Los Angeles") and [History of the National Football League in Los Angeles](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Football_League_in_Los_Angeles "History of the National Football League in Los Angeles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/USC_vs_University_of_Oregon_November_2019_%28cropped%29.png/330px-USC_vs_University_of_Oregon_November_2019_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:USC_vs_University_of_Oregon_November_2019_(cropped).png)

[Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum")

Los Angeles and its metropolitan area are the home of eleven top-level professional sports teams, several of which play in neighboring communities but use Los Angeles in their name. These teams include the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers")[[249]](#cite_note-251) and [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels")[[250]](#cite_note-252) of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB), the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams")[[251]](#cite_note-253) and [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers")[[252]](#cite_note-254) and [Los Angeles Clippers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Clippers "Los Angeles Clippers")[[253]](#cite_note-255) of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA), the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings")[[254]](#cite_note-256) and [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks")[[255]](#cite_note-257) of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL), the [Los Angeles Galaxy](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Galaxy "Los Angeles Galaxy")[[256]](#cite_note-258) and [Los Angeles FC](/wiki/Los_Angeles_FC "Los Angeles FC")[[257]](#cite_note-259) of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS), the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks") of the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA),[[258]](#cite_note-260) the [SoCal Lashings](/wiki/SoCal_Lashings "SoCal Lashings") of [Minor League Cricket](/wiki/Minor_League_Cricket "Minor League Cricket") (MiLC) and the [Los Angeles Knight Riders](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Knight_Riders "Los Angeles Knight Riders") of [Major League Cricket](/wiki/Major_League_Cricket "Major League Cricket") (MLC).[[259]](#cite_note-261)

Other notable sports teams include the [UCLA Bruins](/wiki/UCLA_Bruins "UCLA Bruins") and the [USC Trojans](/wiki/USC_Trojans "USC Trojans") in the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA), both of which are Division I teams in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference").[[260]](#cite_note-262)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Dodger_Stadium_field_from_upper_deck_2015-10-04.jpg/250px-Dodger_Stadium_field_from_upper_deck_2015-10-04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dodger_Stadium_field_from_upper_deck_2015-10-04.jpg)

[Dodger Stadium](/wiki/Dodger_Stadium "Dodger Stadium"), home of the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")

Los Angeles is the second-largest city in the United States but [hosted no NFL team between 1995 and 2015](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Football_League_in_Los_Angeles "History of the National Football League in Los Angeles"). At one time, the Los Angeles area hosted two NFL teams: the [Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") and the [Raiders](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Raiders "Las Vegas Raiders"). Both left the city in 1995, with the Rams moving to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), and the Raiders moving back to their original home of [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"). After 21 seasons in St. Louis, on January 12, 2016, the NFL announced the Rams would be moving back to Los Angeles for the [2016 NFL season](/wiki/2016_NFL_season "2016 NFL season") with its home games played at the [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum") for four seasons.[[261]](#cite_note-263)[[262]](#cite_note-264)[[263]](#cite_note-265) Before 1995, the Rams played their home games in the Coliseum from 1946 to 1979 which made them the first professional sports team to play in Los Angeles, and then moved to [Anaheim Stadium](/wiki/Anaheim_Stadium "Anaheim Stadium") from 1980 until 1994. The [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") announced on January 12, 2017, that they would also relocate back to Los Angeles (the first since its inaugural season in 1960) and become the [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") beginning in the [2017 NFL season](/wiki/2017_NFL_season "2017 NFL season") and played at [Dignity Health Sports Park](/wiki/Dignity_Health_Sports_Park "Dignity Health Sports Park") in [Carson, California](/wiki/Carson,_California "Carson, California"), for three seasons.[[264]](#cite_note-266) The Rams and the Chargers would soon move to the newly built [SoFi Stadium](/wiki/SoFi_Stadium "SoFi Stadium"), located in nearby [Inglewood](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California") during the 2020 season.[[265]](#cite_note-267)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Crypto.com_arena_drone_shot_early_2023.jpg/330px-Crypto.com_arena_drone_shot_early_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crypto.com_arena_drone_shot_early_2023.jpg)

[Crypto.com Arena](/wiki/Crypto.com_Arena "Crypto.com Arena"), home to the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"), [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), and [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks")

Los Angeles boasts a number of sports venues, including [Dodger Stadium](/wiki/Dodger_Stadium "Dodger Stadium"),[[266]](#cite_note-268) the [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum"),[[267]](#cite_note-269) [BMO Stadium](/wiki/BMO_Stadium "BMO Stadium")[[268]](#cite_note-270) and [Crypto.com Arena](/wiki/Crypto.com_Arena "Crypto.com Arena").[[269]](#cite_note-271) The [Kia Forum](/wiki/Kia_Forum "Kia Forum"), SoFi Stadium, Dignity Health Sports Park, the [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(stadium) "Rose Bowl (stadium)"), [Angel Stadium](/wiki/Angel_Stadium "Angel Stadium"), [Honda Center](/wiki/Honda_Center "Honda Center"), and [Intuit Dome](/wiki/Intuit_Dome "Intuit Dome") are also in adjacent cities and cities in Los Angeles's metropolitan area.[[270]](#cite_note-272)

Los Angeles has twice hosted the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"): in [1932](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics") and in [1984](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics"), and will host the games for a third time in [2028](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics").[[271]](#cite_note-273) Los Angeles will be the third city after London ([1908](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), [1948](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics") and [2012](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics")) and Paris ([1900](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics"), [1924](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics") and [2024](/wiki/2024_Summer_Olympics "2024 Summer Olympics")) to host the Olympic Games three times. Los Angeles has [submitted 10 bids](/wiki/Los_Angeles_bids_for_the_Summer_Olympics "Los Angeles bids for the Summer Olympics") to host Olympic events to the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee"), more than any other city in the world.[[272]](#cite_note-274) When the tenth Olympic Games were hosted in 1932, the former 10th Street was renamed Olympic Blvd. Los Angeles also hosted the [Deaflympics](/wiki/Deaflympics "Deaflympics") in [1985](/wiki/1985_Summer_Deaflympics "1985 Summer Deaflympics")[[273]](#cite_note-275) and [Special Olympics World Summer Games](/wiki/Special_Olympics_World_Games "Special Olympics World Games") in [2015](/wiki/2015_Special_Olympics_World_Summer_Games "2015 Special Olympics World Summer Games").[[274]](#cite_note-276)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/LAFC_vs_Philadelphia_Union_%282018%29_by_Subashwilfred_%2820180630233148%29.jpg/250px-LAFC_vs_Philadelphia_Union_%282018%29_by_Subashwilfred_%2820180630233148%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:LAFC_vs_Philadelphia_Union_(2018)_by_Subashwilfred_(20180630233148).jpg)

[BMO Stadium](/wiki/BMO_Stadium "BMO Stadium"), home of [Los Angeles FC](/wiki/Los_Angeles_FC "Los Angeles FC") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer")

Eight NFL [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") have also been held in the city and its surrounding areas - two at the Memorial Coliseum ([the first Super Bowl, I](/wiki/Super_Bowl_I "Super Bowl I") and [VII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_VII "Super Bowl VII")), five at the Rose Bowl in suburban [Pasadena](/wiki/Pasadena,_California "Pasadena, California") ([XI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XI "Super Bowl XI"), [XIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XIV "Super Bowl XIV"), [XVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XVII "Super Bowl XVII"), [XXI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXI "Super Bowl XXI"), and [XXVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXVII "Super Bowl XXVII")), and one at the suburban [Inglewood](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California") ([LVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI "Super Bowl LVI")).[[275]](#cite_note-277) The Rose Bowl also hosts an annual and highly prestigious [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football") game called the [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_game "Rose Bowl game"), which happens every New Year's Day.

Los Angeles also hosted eight [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") soccer games at the [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(stadium) "Rose Bowl (stadium)") in [1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), including the [final](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_Final "1994 FIFA World Cup Final"), where [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") won. The Rose Bowl also hosted four matches in the [1999 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/1999_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "1999 FIFA Women's World Cup"), including the [final](/wiki/1999_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup_Final "1999 FIFA Women's World Cup Final"), where the [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_soccer_team "United States women's national soccer team") won against [China](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_football_team "China women's national football team") on penalty kicks. This was the game where [Brandi Chastain](/wiki/Brandi_Chastain "Brandi Chastain") took her shirt off after she scored the tournament-winning penalty kick, creating an iconic image. Los Angeles will be one of eleven U.S. host cities for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup") with matches set to be held at [SoFi Stadium](/wiki/SoFi_Stadium "SoFi Stadium").[[276]](#cite_note-278)

Los Angeles is one of six North American cities to have won championships in all five of its major leagues (MLB, NFL, NHL, NBA, and MLS), having completed the feat with the Kings' [2012 Stanley Cup title](/wiki/2012_Stanley_Cup_Finals "2012 Stanley Cup Finals").[[277]](#cite_note-279)

## Government

Main article: [Government of Los Angeles](/wiki/Government_of_Los_Angeles "Government of Los Angeles")

Further information: [List of elected officials in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_elected_officials_in_Los_Angeles "List of elected officials in Los Angeles")

See also: [Government of Los Angeles County](/wiki/Government_of_Los_Angeles_County "Government of Los Angeles County")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/City_Hall%2C_LA%2C_CA%2C_jjron_22.03.2012_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-City_Hall%2C_LA%2C_CA%2C_jjron_22.03.2012_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Hall,_LA,_CA,_jjron_22.03.2012_(cropped).jpg)

[Los Angeles City Hall](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Hall "Los Angeles City Hall"), built in 1928, houses the [Mayor of Los Angeles](/wiki/Mayor_of_Los_Angeles "Mayor of Los Angeles") and the [Los Angeles City Council](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Council "Los Angeles City Council").

Los Angeles is a [charter city](/wiki/Charter_city "Charter city") as opposed to a [general law city](/wiki/General_law_city "General law city"). The current charter was adopted on June 8, 1999, and has been amended many times.[[278]](#cite_note-280) The [elected government](/wiki/List_of_elected_officials_in_Los_Angeles "List of elected officials in Los Angeles") consists of the [Los Angeles City Council](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Council "Los Angeles City Council") and the [mayor of Los Angeles](/wiki/Mayor_of_Los_Angeles "Mayor of Los Angeles"), which operate under a [mayor–council government](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government"), as well as the [city attorney](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Attorney "Los Angeles City Attorney") (not to be confused with the [district attorney](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_District_Attorney "Los Angeles County District Attorney"), a county office) and [controller](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Controller "Los Angeles City Controller"). The mayor is [Karen Bass](/wiki/Karen_Bass "Karen Bass").[[279]](#cite_note-281) There are [15 city council districts](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_Council "Los Angeles City Council").

The city has many departments and appointed officers, including the [Los Angeles Police Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") (LAPD),[[280]](#cite_note-282) the [Los Angeles Board of Police Commissioners](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Board_of_Police_Commissioners "Los Angeles Board of Police Commissioners"),[[281]](#cite_note-283) the [Los Angeles Fire Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Fire_Department "Los Angeles Fire Department") (LAFD),[[282]](#cite_note-284) the [Housing Authority of the City of Los Angeles](/wiki/Housing_Authority_of_the_City_of_Los_Angeles "Housing Authority of the City of Los Angeles") (HACLA),[[283]](#cite_note-285) the [Los Angeles Department of Transportation](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Department_of_Transportation "Los Angeles Department of Transportation") (LADOT),[[284]](#cite_note-286) and the [Los Angeles Public Library](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Public_Library "Los Angeles Public Library") (LAPL).[[285]](#cite_note-287)

The charter of the City of Los Angeles, ratified by voters in 1999, created a system of advisory neighborhood councils that would represent the diversity of stakeholders, defined as those who live, work, or own property in the neighborhood. The neighborhood councils are relatively autonomous and spontaneous in that they identify their own boundaries, establish their own bylaws, and elect their own officers. There are about 90 neighborhood councils.

Residents of Los Angeles elect [supervisors](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Board_of_Supervisors "Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors") for the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th supervisorial districts.

### Federal and state representation

In the [California State Assembly](/wiki/California_State_Assembly "California State Assembly"), Los Angeles is split between fourteen districts.[[286]](#cite_note-288) In the [California State Senate](/wiki/California_State_Senate "California State Senate"), the city is split between eight districts.[[287]](#cite_note-289) In the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), it is split among nine congressional districts.[[288]](#cite_note-290)

## Crime

Main article: [Crime in Los Angeles](/wiki/Crime_in_Los_Angeles "Crime in Los Angeles")

See also: [List of criminal gangs in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_criminal_gangs_in_Los_Angeles "List of criminal gangs in Los Angeles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/May_Day_Immigration_March_LA66.jpg/250px-May_Day_Immigration_March_LA66.jpg)](/wiki/File:May_Day_Immigration_March_LA66.jpg)

The [LAPD](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") on [May Day](/wiki/May_Day "May Day") 2006 in front of the new Caltrans District 7 Headquarters

In 1992, the city of Los Angeles recorded 1,092 murders.[[291]](#cite_note-293) Los Angeles experienced a significant decline in crime in the 1990s and late 2000s and reached a 50-year low in 2009 with 314 homicides.[[292]](#cite_note-294)[[293]](#cite_note-295) This is a rate of 7.8 per 100,000—down from 1980, when it was 34.2.[[c]](#cite_note-296)[[294]](#cite_note-297)[[295]](#cite_note-298) In 2021, murders rose to their highest rate (8.5) since 2008, although by 2024 (6.1), the spike had receded.[[296]](#cite_note-299)

In 2015, it was revealed that the LAPD had been under-reporting a category of crime between 2005 and 2012, making the rate in the city appear lower than it was for that period.[[297]](#cite_note-300)[[298]](#cite_note-301)

The [Dragna crime family](/wiki/Los_Angeles_crime_family "Los Angeles crime family") and [Mickey Cohen](/wiki/Mickey_Cohen "Mickey Cohen") dominated organized crime in the city during the [Prohibition era](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States")[[299]](#cite_note-mafiaLA-302) and reached its peak during the 1940s and 1950s with the "Battle of Sunset Strip" as part of the [American Mafia](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia"), but has gradually declined since then with the rise of various black and [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") gangs in the late 1960s and early 1970s.[[299]](#cite_note-mafiaLA-302)

According to the [Los Angeles Police Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department"), the city is home to 45,000 gang members, organized into 450 gangs.[[300]](#cite_note-303) Among them are the [Crips](/wiki/Crips "Crips") and [Bloods](/wiki/Bloods "Bloods"), which are both African American street gangs that originated in the [South Los Angeles](/wiki/South_Los_Angeles "South Los Angeles") region. Latino street gangs such as the [Sureños](/wiki/Sure%C3%B1os "Sureños"), a Mexican American street gang, and [Mara Salvatrucha](/wiki/Mara_Salvatrucha "Mara Salvatrucha"), which has mainly members of [Salvadoran](/wiki/Salvadoran_American "Salvadoran American") descent, as well as other [Central American](/wiki/Central_American "Central American") descendants, all originated in Los Angeles. This has led to the city being referred to as the "Gang Capital of America".[[301]](#cite_note-304)

* [Occidental College](/wiki/Occidental_College "Occidental College") ("Oxy")[[318]](#cite_note-321)
* [Otis College of Art and Design](/wiki/Otis_College_of_Art_and_Design "Otis College of Art and Design") (Otis)[[319]](#cite_note-322)
* [Southern California Institute of Architecture](/wiki/Southern_California_Institute_of_Architecture "Southern California Institute of Architecture") (SCI-Arc)[[320]](#cite_note-323)
* [Southwestern Law School](/wiki/Southwestern_Law_School "Southwestern Law School")[[321]](#cite_note-324)
* [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") (USC)[[322]](#cite_note-325)
* [Woodbury University](/wiki/Woodbury_University "Woodbury University")[[323]](#cite_note-326)

The community college system consists of nine campuses governed by the trustees of the Los Angeles Community College District:

* [East Los Angeles College](/wiki/East_Los_Angeles_College "East Los Angeles College") (ELAC)[[324]](#cite_note-327)
* [Los Angeles City College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_City_College "Los Angeles City College") (LACC)[[325]](#cite_note-328)
* [Los Angeles Harbor College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Harbor_College "Los Angeles Harbor College")[[326]](#cite_note-329)
* [Los Angeles Mission College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Mission_College "Los Angeles Mission College")[[327]](#cite_note-330)
* [Los Angeles Pierce College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Pierce_College "Los Angeles Pierce College")[[328]](#cite_note-331)
* [Los Angeles Valley College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Valley_College "Los Angeles Valley College") (LAVC)[[329]](#cite_note-332)
* [Los Angeles Southwest College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Southwest_College "Los Angeles Southwest College")[[330]](#cite_note-333)
* [Los Angeles Trade-Technical College](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Trade-Technical_College "Los Angeles Trade-Technical College")[[331]](#cite_note-334)
* [West Los Angeles College](/wiki/West_Los_Angeles_College "West Los Angeles College")[[332]](#cite_note-335)

There are numerous additional colleges and universities outside the city limits in the Greater Los Angeles area, including the [Claremont Colleges](/wiki/Claremont_Colleges "Claremont Colleges") consortium, which includes the most selective liberal arts colleges in the U.S., and the [California Institute of Technology](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology") (Caltech), one of the top STEM-focused research institutions in the world.

### Schools

See also: [Los Angeles County, California § Education](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California#Education "Los Angeles County, California"); and [List of high schools in Los Angeles County, California](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Los_Angeles_County,_California "List of high schools in Los Angeles County, California")

[Los Angeles Unified School District](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Unified_School_District "Los Angeles Unified School District") serves almost all of the city of Los Angeles, as well as several surrounding communities, with a student population of around 800,000.[[333]](#cite_note-336) After [Proposition 13](/wiki/California_Proposition_13_(1978) "California Proposition 13 (1978)") was approved in 1978, urban school districts had considerable trouble with funding. LAUSD has become known for its underfunded, overcrowded, and poorly maintained campuses, although its 162 Magnet schools help compete with local private schools.

Several small sections of Los Angeles are in the [Inglewood Unified School District](/wiki/Inglewood_Unified_School_District "Inglewood Unified School District"),[[334]](#cite_note-337) and the [Las Virgenes Unified School District](/wiki/Las_Virgenes_Unified_School_District "Las Virgenes Unified School District").[[335]](#cite_note-338) The Los Angeles County Office of Education operates the [Los Angeles County High School for the Arts](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_High_School_for_the_Arts "Los Angeles County High School for the Arts").

## Media

Main article: [Media in Los Angeles](/wiki/Media_in_Los_Angeles "Media in Los Angeles")

See also: [List of television shows set in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_television_shows_set_in_Los_Angeles "List of television shows set in Los Angeles"), [List of films set in Los Angeles](/wiki/List_of_films_set_in_Los_Angeles "List of films set in Los Angeles"), and [List of Los Angeles television stations](/wiki/List_of_Los_Angeles_television_stations "List of Los Angeles television stations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Hollywood_Sign_%28Zuschnitt%29.jpg/250px-Hollywood_Sign_%28Zuschnitt%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollywood_Sign_(Zuschnitt).jpg)

The [Hollywood Sign](/wiki/Hollywood_Sign "Hollywood Sign") is a prominent symbol of the [American film industry](/wiki/American_film_industry "American film industry").

The Los Angeles metro area is the second-largest broadcast [designated market area](/wiki/Designated_market_area "Designated market area") in the U.S. (after [New York](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City")) with 5,431,140 homes (4.956% of the U.S.), which is served by a wide variety of local [AM](/wiki/Media_in_Los_Angeles#AM "Media in Los Angeles") and [FM](/wiki/Media_in_Los_Angeles#FM "Media in Los Angeles") radio and [television](/wiki/Media_in_Los_Angeles#Television "Media in Los Angeles") stations. Los Angeles and New York City are the only two media markets to have seven [VHF](/wiki/VHF "VHF") allocations assigned to them.[[336]](#cite_note-339)

The major daily English-language newspaper in the area is the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*.[[337]](#cite_note-340) *[La Opinión](/wiki/La_Opini%C3%B3n "La Opinión")* is the city's major daily Spanish-language paper.[[338]](#cite_note-341) *[The Korea Times](/wiki/The_Korea_Times_(Los_Angeles) "The Korea Times (Los Angeles)")* is the city's major daily [Korean-language](/wiki/Korean-language "Korean-language") paper while *[The World Journal](/wiki/The_World_Journal "The World Journal")* is the city and county's major Chinese newspaper. The *[Los Angeles Sentinel](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sentinel "Los Angeles Sentinel")* is the city's major African-American weekly paper, boasting the largest African-American readership in the Western United States.[[339]](#cite_note-342) *[Investor's Business Daily](/wiki/Investor%27s_Business_Daily "Investor's Business Daily")* is distributed from its LA corporate offices, which are headquartered in Playa del Rey.[[340]](#cite_note-343)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Los_Angeles_Times_Building_07.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Times_Building_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Times_Building_07.jpg)

The former *[LA Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* headquarters

As part of the region's aforementioned creative industry, the Big Five major broadcast television networks, [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), [FOX](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company"), [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), and [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW"), all have production facilities and offices throughout various areas of Los Angeles. All four major broadcast television networks, plus major Spanish-language networks [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") and [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision"), also own and operate stations that both serve the Los Angeles market and serve as each network's West Coast [flagship station](/wiki/Flagship_station "Flagship station"): ABC's [KABC-TV](/wiki/KABC-TV "KABC-TV") (Channel 7),[[341]](#cite_note-344) CBS's [KCBS-TV](/wiki/KCBS-TV "KCBS-TV") (Channel 2), Fox's [KTTV](/wiki/KTTV "KTTV")-TV (Channel 11),[[342]](#cite_note-345) NBC's [KNBC](/wiki/KNBC "KNBC")-TV (Channel 4),[[343]](#cite_note-346) The CW's [KTLA](/wiki/KTLA "KTLA")-TV (Channel 5), MyNetworkTV's [KCOP](/wiki/KCOP "KCOP")-TV (Channel 13), Telemundo's [KVEA](/wiki/KVEA "KVEA")-TV (Channel 52), and Univision's [KMEX-TV](/wiki/KMEX-DT "KMEX-DT") (Channel 34). The region also has four [PBS](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") member stations, with [KCET](/wiki/KCET "KCET") re-joining the network as a secondary affiliate in August 2019, after spending the previous eight years as the nation's largest independent public television station. [KTBN](/wiki/KTBN "KTBN") (Channel 40) is the [flagship station](/wiki/Flagship_station "Flagship station") of the religious [Trinity Broadcasting Network](/wiki/Trinity_Broadcasting_Network "Trinity Broadcasting Network"), based out of [Santa Ana](/wiki/Santa_Ana,_California "Santa Ana, California"). A variety of independent television stations, such as [KCAL-TV](/wiki/KCAL-TV "KCAL-TV") (Channel 9), also operate in the area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Paramountpicturesmelrosegate_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Paramountpicturesmelrosegate_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paramountpicturesmelrosegate_(cropped).jpg)

[Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") Studios

There are also a number of smaller regional newspapers, alternative weeklies and magazines, including the *[Los Angeles Register](/wiki/Freedom_Communications "Freedom Communications")*, Los Angeles Community News, (which focuses on coverage of the greater Los Angeles area), *[Los Angeles Daily News](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Daily_News "Los Angeles Daily News")* (which focuses coverage on the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley")), *[LA Weekly](/wiki/LA_Weekly "LA Weekly")*, *[L.A. Record](/wiki/L.A._Record "L.A. Record")* (which focuses coverage on the music scene in the [Greater Los Angeles Area](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles_Area "Greater Los Angeles Area")), *Los Angeles Magazine*, the *[Los Angeles Business Journal](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Business_Journal "Los Angeles Business Journal")*, the *Los Angeles Daily Journal* (legal industry paper), *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")*, *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* (both entertainment industry papers), and *[Los Angeles Downtown News](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Downtown_News "Los Angeles Downtown News")*.[[344]](#cite_note-347) In addition to the major papers, numerous local periodicals serve immigrant communities in their native languages, including Armenian, English, Korean, Persian, Russian, Chinese, Japanese, Hebrew, and Arabic. Many cities adjacent to Los Angeles also have their own daily newspapers whose coverage and availability overlap with certain Los Angeles neighborhoods. Examples include *The [Daily Breeze](/wiki/Daily_Breeze "Daily Breeze")* (serving the [South Bay](/wiki/South_Bay,_Los_Angeles "South Bay, Los Angeles")), and *The [Long Beach Press-Telegram](/wiki/Long_Beach_Press-Telegram "Long Beach Press-Telegram")*.

Los Angeles arts, culture and nightlife news is also covered by a number of local and national online guides, including *Time Out Los Angeles*, *[Thrillist](/wiki/Thrillist "Thrillist")*, *Kristin's List*, *DailyCandy*, *Diversity News Magazine*, *LAist*, and *Flavorpill*.[[345]](#cite_note-348)[[346]](#cite_note-349)[[347]](#cite_note-350)[[348]](#cite_note-351)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in Los Angeles](/wiki/Transportation_in_Los_Angeles "Transportation in Los Angeles")

#### Freeways

Main article: [Southern California freeways](/wiki/Southern_California_freeways "Southern California freeways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Los_Angeles_-_Echangeur_autoroute_110_105_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Los_Angeles_-_Echangeur_autoroute_110_105_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_-_Echangeur_autoroute_110_105_(cropped).JPG)

[Judge Harry Pregerson Interchange](/wiki/Judge_Harry_Pregerson_Interchange "Judge Harry Pregerson Interchange"), connecting the [Century Freeway (I-105)](/wiki/Interstate_105_(California) "Interstate 105 (California)") and the [Harbor Freeway (I-110)](/wiki/Interstate_110_and_State_Route_110_(California) "Interstate 110 and State Route 110 (California)") in [South LA](/wiki/South_Los_Angeles "South Los Angeles")

The city and the rest of the [Los Angeles metropolitan area](/wiki/Los_Angeles_metropolitan_area "Los Angeles metropolitan area") are served by an extensive network of freeways and highways. [Texas Transportation Institute](/wiki/Texas_Transportation_Institute "Texas Transportation Institute")'s annual Urban Mobility Report ranked Los Angeles area roads the most congested in the United States in 2019, as measured by annual delay per traveler, area residents experiencing a cumulative average of 119 hours waiting in traffic that year.[[349]](#cite_note-352) Los Angeles was followed by San Francisco/[Oakland](/wiki/Oakland "Oakland"), Washington, D.C., and [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"). Despite the congestion in the city, the mean daily travel time for commuters in Los Angeles is shorter than in other major cities, including New York City, [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), and Chicago. Los Angeles's mean travel time for work commutes in 2006 was 29.2 minutes, similar to those of San Francisco and Washington, D.C.[[350]](#cite_note-ACS2006-353)

The major highways that connect LA to the rest of the nation include [I-5](/wiki/Interstate_5_in_California "Interstate 5 in California"), which runs south through [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") to the [Mexican border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border") and north through [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento,_California "Sacramento, California"), [Portland](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), and [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle") to the [Canadian border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border"); [I-10](/wiki/Interstate_10_in_California "Interstate 10 in California"), the southernmost east–west, coast-to-coast [interstate highway](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") in the United States, going to [Jacksonville](/wiki/Jacksonville,_Florida "Jacksonville, Florida"); and [US 101](/wiki/U.S._Route_101_in_California "U.S. Route 101 in California"), which heads to the [California Central Coast](/wiki/Central_Coast_(California) "Central Coast (California)"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), the [Redwood Empire](/wiki/North_Coast_(California) "North Coast (California)"), and the [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon") and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") coasts.

#### Buses

Main article: [Los Angeles Metro](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro "Los Angeles Metro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/LA_Metro_200_bus_stop_on_Alvarado_Street.jpg/250px-LA_Metro_200_bus_stop_on_Alvarado_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:LA_Metro_200_bus_stop_on_Alvarado_Street.jpg)

[Los Angeles Metro Bus](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Bus "Los Angeles Metro Bus") operated by the [Los Angeles Metro](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro "Los Angeles Metro").

The [Los Angeles Metro](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro "Los Angeles Metro") and other regional agencies provide a comprehensive bus system that covers Los Angeles County. While the [Los Angeles Department of Transportation](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Department_of_Transportation "Los Angeles Department of Transportation") is responsible for contracting local and commuter bus services primarily within the city limits of Los Angeles and several immediate neighboring municipalities in southwest Los Angeles County,[[351]](#cite_note-354) the largest bus system in the city is operated by Metro.[[352]](#cite_note-355) Called [Los Angeles Metro Bus](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Bus "Los Angeles Metro Bus"), the system consists of 117 routes (excluding Metro Busway) throughout Los Angeles and neighboring cities primarily in southwestern Los Angeles County, with most routes following along a particular street in the city's street grid and run to or through downtown Los Angeles.[[353]](#cite_note-Schedules_-_LA_Metro-356) As of the third quarter of 2023, the system had an average ridership of approximately 692,500 per weekday, with a total of 197,950,700 riders in 2022.[[354]](#cite_note-APTAdaily-357) Metro also runs two [Metro Busway](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Busway "Los Angeles Metro Busway") lines, the [G](/wiki/G_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "G Line (Los Angeles Metro)") and [J](/wiki/J_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "J Line (Los Angeles Metro)") lines, which are [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") lines with stops and frequencies similar to those of Los Angeles's light rail system.

There are also smaller regional public transit systems that mainly serve specific cities or regions in Los Angeles County. For example, the [Big Blue Bus](/wiki/Big_Blue_Bus "Big Blue Bus") provides extensive bus service in Santa Monica and western Los Angeles County, while [Foothill Transit](/wiki/Foothill_Transit "Foothill Transit") focuses on routes in the San Gabriel Valley in southeast Los Angeles County, although it operates the [Silver Streak](/wiki/Silver_Streak_(bus) "Silver Streak (bus)") bus rapid transit into downtown Los Angeles. [Los Angeles World Airports](/wiki/Los_Angeles_World_Airports "Los Angeles World Airports") also runs two frequent [FlyAway](/wiki/FlyAway_(bus) "FlyAway (bus)") express bus routes (via freeways) from [Los Angeles Union Station](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Union_Station "Los Angeles Union Station") and [Van Nuys](/wiki/Van_Nuys "Van Nuys") to Los Angeles International Airport.[[355]](#cite_note-358)

While cash is accepted on all buses, the primary payment method for Los Angeles Metro Bus, Metro Busway, and 27 other regional bus agencies is a [TAP card](/wiki/TAP_card "TAP card"), a contactless stored-value card.[[356]](#cite_note-359) According to the 2016 American Community Survey, 9.2% of working Los Angeles (city) residents made the journey to work via public transportation.[[357]](#cite_note-360)

#### Rail

Main articles: [Los Angeles Metro Rail](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail") and [Metrolink (California)](/wiki/Metrolink_(California) "Metrolink (California)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Los_Angeles_Metro_System_Map.png/250px-Los_Angeles_Metro_System_Map.png)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Metro_System_Map.png)

Map of the Los Angeles Metro Rail system (as of September 19, 2025).

The Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority also operates a [subway](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") and [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") system across Los Angeles and its county. The system is called [Los Angeles Metro Rail](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail") and consists of the [B](/wiki/B_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "B Line (Los Angeles Metro)") and [D](/wiki/D_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "D Line (Los Angeles Metro)") subway lines, as well as the [A](/wiki/A_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "A Line (Los Angeles Metro)"), [C](/wiki/C_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "C Line (Los Angeles Metro)"), [E](/wiki/E_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "E Line (Los Angeles Metro)"), and [K](/wiki/K_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "K Line (Los Angeles Metro)") light rail lines.[[353]](#cite_note-Schedules_-_LA_Metro-356) TAP cards are required for all Metro Rail trips.[[358]](#cite_note-361) As of the third quarter of 2023, the [city's subway system](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail") is the [ninth busiest in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_rapid_transit_systems_by_ridership "List of United States rapid transit systems by ridership"), and its light rail system is the country's second [busiest](/wiki/List_of_United_States_light_rail_systems_by_ridership "List of United States light rail systems by ridership").[[354]](#cite_note-APTAdaily-357) In 2022, the system had a ridership of 57,299,800, or about 189,200 per weekday, in the third quarter of 2023.[[354]](#cite_note-APTAdaily-357)

Since the opening of the first line, the A Line, in 1990, the system has been extended significantly, with more extensions currently in progress. Today, the system serves numerous areas across the county on 107.4 mi (172.8 km) of rail, including [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California"), [Pasadena](/wiki/Pasadena "Pasadena"), [Santa Monica](/wiki/Santa_Monica,_California "Santa Monica, California"), [Norwalk](/wiki/Norwalk,_California "Norwalk, California"), [El Segundo](/wiki/El_Segundo,_California "El Segundo, California"), [North Hollywood](/wiki/North_Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "North Hollywood, Los Angeles"), [Inglewood](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California"), and [downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles"). As of 2023, there are 101 stations in the Metro Rail system.[[359]](#cite_note-362)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Metrolink_System_Map.png/250px-Metrolink_System_Map.png)](/wiki/File:Metrolink_System_Map.png)

[Metrolink](/wiki/Metrolink_(California) "Metrolink (California)") passenger rail map, which stretches from [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_California "Lancaster, California") to [Oceanside](/wiki/Oceanside,_California "Oceanside, California"), with [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Los_Angeles) "Union Station (Los Angeles)") as the central hub.

Los Angeles is also the center of its county's [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") system, [Metrolink](/wiki/Metrolink_(California) "Metrolink (California)"), which links Los Angeles to Ventura, Orange, Riverside, San Bernardino, and San Diego Counties. The system consists of eight lines and 69 stations operating on 545.6 miles (878.1 kilometers) of track.[[360]](#cite_note-363) Metrolink averages 42,600 trips per weekday, the busiest line being the [San Bernardino Line](/wiki/San_Bernardino_Line "San Bernardino Line").[[361]](#cite_note-APTA2-364) Apart from Metrolink, Los Angeles is also connected to other cities by intercity passenger trains from [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") on five different lines.[[362]](#cite_note-365) One of the lines is the [Pacific Surfliner](/wiki/Pacific_Surfliner "Pacific Surfliner") route which operates multiple daily round trips between [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") and [San Luis Obispo, California](/wiki/San_Luis_Obispo,_California "San Luis Obispo, California") through Union Station.[[363]](#cite_note-366) It is Amtrak's busiest line outside the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor").[[364]](#cite_note-367)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Los_Angeles_Union_Station_22.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Union_Station_22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Union_Station_22.jpg)

[Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Los_Angeles) "Union Station (Los Angeles)") is served by [Amtrak California](/wiki/Amtrak_California "Amtrak California"), [Metrolink](/wiki/Metrolink_(California) "Metrolink (California)"), and [Metro Rail](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail").

The main rail station in the city is [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Los_Angeles) "Union Station (Los Angeles)") which opened in 1939, and it is the largest passenger rail terminal in the [Western United States](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States").[[365]](#cite_note-sbcs-368) The station is a major regional [train station](/wiki/Train_station "Train station") for [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), [Metrolink](/wiki/Metrolink_(California) "Metrolink (California)") and [Metro Rail](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail"). The station is Amtrak's fifth busiest station, having 1.4 million Amtrak boardings and de-boardings in 2019.[[366]](#cite_note-369) Union Station also offers access to Metro Bus, [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), LAX FlyAway, and other buses from different agencies.[[367]](#cite_note-370)

#### Airports

Main article: [List of airports in the Los Angeles area](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_Los_Angeles_area "List of airports in the Los Angeles area")

See also: [Los Angeles International Airport in popular culture](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport_in_popular_culture "Los Angeles International Airport in popular culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/TheThemeBuildingLosAngeles_%28cropped2%29.jpg/250px-TheThemeBuildingLosAngeles_%28cropped2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TheThemeBuildingLosAngeles_(cropped2).jpg)

[Los Angeles Intl. Airport](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport") (LAX) is the [eighth-busiest airport in the world](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "World's busiest airports by passenger traffic").

The main international and domestic airport serving Los Angeles is [Los Angeles International Airport](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport"), commonly referred to by its airport code, LAX.[[368]](#cite_note-371) It is located on the Westside of Los Angeles near the [Sofi Stadium](/wiki/SoFi_Stadium "SoFi Stadium") in Inglewood.

Other major nearby commercial airports include:

* [Ontario International Airport](/wiki/Ontario_International_Airport "Ontario International Airport"), owned by the city of Ontario, serves the Inland Empire.[[369]](#cite_note-372)
* [Hollywood Burbank Airport](/wiki/Hollywood_Burbank_Airport "Hollywood Burbank Airport"), jointly owned by the cities of Burbank, Glendale, and Pasadena. Formerly known as Bob Hope Airport and Burbank Airport, it is the closest airport to downtown Los Angeles and serves the San Fernando, San Gabriel, and Antelope Valleys.[[370]](#cite_note-373)
* [Long Beach Airport](/wiki/Long_Beach_Airport "Long Beach Airport") serves the Long Beach/Harbor area.[[371]](#cite_note-374)
* [John Wayne Airport](/wiki/John_Wayne_Airport "John Wayne Airport") of Orange County.

One of the world's busiest general-aviation airports is also in Los Angeles: [Van Nuys Airport](/wiki/Van_Nuys_Airport "Van Nuys Airport").[[372]](#cite_note-375)

#### Seaports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Vincent_Thomas_bridge.San_Pedro.JPG/250px-Vincent_Thomas_bridge.San_Pedro.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vincent_Thomas_bridge.San_Pedro.JPG)

[Vincent Thomas Bridge](/wiki/Vincent_Thomas_Bridge "Vincent Thomas Bridge") at [Terminal Island](/wiki/Terminal_Island "Terminal Island") in the [Port of Los Angeles](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles")

The [Port of Los Angeles](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles") is in [San Pedro Bay](/wiki/San_Pedro_Bay_(California) "San Pedro Bay (California)") in the [San Pedro](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles "San Pedro, Los Angeles") neighborhood, approximately 20 miles (32 km) south of downtown. Also called Los Angeles Harbor and WORLDPORT LA, the port complex occupies 7,500 acres (30 km2) of land and water along 43 miles (69 km) of waterfront. It adjoins the separate [Port of Long Beach](/wiki/Port_of_Long_Beach "Port of Long Beach").[[373]](#cite_note-376)

The sea ports of the [Port of Los Angeles](/wiki/Port_of_Los_Angeles "Port of Los Angeles") and [Port of Long Beach](/wiki/Port_of_Long_Beach "Port of Long Beach") together make up the *Los Angeles/Long Beach Harbor*.[[374]](#cite_note-377)[[375]](#cite_note-378) Together, both ports are the fifth busiest [container](/wiki/Container_(cargo) "Container (cargo)") port in the world, with a trade volume of over 14.2 million [TEU's](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit") in 2008.[[376]](#cite_note-379) Singly, the Port of Los Angeles is the busiest container port in the United States and the largest cruise ship center on the [West Coast of the United States](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") – The Port of Los Angeles's World Cruise Center served about 590,000 passengers in 2014.[[377]](#cite_note-380)

There are also smaller, non-industrial harbors along Los Angeles's coastline. The port includes four bridges: the [Vincent Thomas Bridge](/wiki/Vincent_Thomas_Bridge "Vincent Thomas Bridge"), [Henry Ford Bridge](/wiki/Henry_Ford_Bridge "Henry Ford Bridge"), [Long Beach International Gateway Bridge](/wiki/Long_Beach_International_Gateway "Long Beach International Gateway"), and [Commodore Schuyler F. Heim Bridge](/wiki/Commodore_Schuyler_F._Heim_Bridge "Commodore Schuyler F. Heim Bridge"). Passenger ferry service from San Pedro to the city of [Avalon](/wiki/Avalon,_California "Avalon, California") (and [Two Harbors](/wiki/Two_Harbors,_California "Two Harbors, California")) on [Santa Catalina Island](/wiki/Santa_Catalina_Island_(California) "Santa Catalina Island (California)") is provided by Catalina Express.

## Further reading

For a more comprehensive list, see [Bibliography of Los Angeles](/wiki/Bibliography_of_Los_Angeles "Bibliography of Los Angeles").

### General

* Abu-Lughod, Janet L. *New York, Chicago, Los Angeles: America's global cities* (U of Minnesota Press, 1999). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-8166-3336-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8166-3336-4 "Special:BookSources/978-0-8166-3336-4"). [online](https://archive.org/details/newyorkchicagolo0000abul)
* Holli, Melvin G., and Jones, Peter d'A., eds. *Biographical Dictionary of American Mayors, 1820–1980* (Greenwood Press, 1981) provides short scholarly biographies of each of the city's mayors from 1820 to 1980. [online](https://archive.org/details/biographicaldict0000unse_r8s1); see index at p. 409 for list.
* [Carey McWilliams](/wiki/Carey_McWilliams_(journalist) "Carey McWilliams (journalist)") (2009). [*Southern California: An Island on the Land*](https://archive.org/details/southerncaliforn00mcwi) (9th ed.). Peregrine Smith. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-87905-007-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-87905-007-8 "Special:BookSources/978-0-87905-007-8").
* [Richard White](/wiki/Richard_White_(historian) "Richard White (historian)") (1991). [*It's Your Misfortune and None of My Own: A New History of the American West*](/wiki/It%27s_Your_Misfortune_and_None_of_My_Own "It's Your Misfortune and None of My Own"). University of Oklahoma Press. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-8061-2567-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8061-2567-1 "Special:BookSources/978-0-8061-2567-1").
* [David Rieff](/wiki/David_Rieff "David Rieff") (1992). *Los Angeles: Capital of the Third World*. Touchstone. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-671-79210-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-671-79210-7 "Special:BookSources/978-0-671-79210-7").
* [Peter Theroux](/wiki/Peter_Theroux "Peter Theroux") (1994). [*Translating LA: A Tour of the Rainbow City*](https://archive.org/details/translatingla00pete). Norton. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-393-31394-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-393-31394-9 "Special:BookSources/978-0-393-31394-9").
* [Paul Glover](/wiki/Paul_Glover_(activist) "Paul Glover (activist)") (1995). *Los Angeles: A History of the Future*. Greenplanners. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-9622911-0-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-9622911-0-4 "Special:BookSources/978-0-9622911-0-4").
* Leonard Pitt & Dale Pitt (2000). *Los Angeles A to Z: An Encyclopedia of the City and County*. Berkeley: University of California Press. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-520-20530-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-520-20530-7 "Special:BookSources/978-0-520-20530-7").
* Eva Kassens Noor (2020). [*Los Angeles and the Summer Olympic Games*](https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-030-38553-8). SpringerBriefs in Geography. [MIT Press](/wiki/MIT_Press "MIT Press"). [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1007/978-3-030-38553-8](https://doi.org/10.1007%2F978-3-030-38553-8). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-030-38553-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-030-38553-8 "Special:BookSources/978-3-030-38553-8").
